Cassia (qiddah, H6916, a cinnamon-related spice from South Asia or East Africa) and fragrant calamus (qaneh hatov, sweet cane/calamus, also from South/Southeast Asia) were luxury aromatics used in incense, medicine, and perfumery. These goods arrived via the Arabia-overland route.
27.20: 'Dedan traded with you in saddlecloths for riding' — a second reference to Dedan (cf. v.15), this time specifically for riding accessories. The Arabian horse-gear trade supplemented the Armenian horse supply: horses needed saddle cloths, bridles, and tack. Arabian craftsmen were known for their leather and textile work for equestrian equipment.
27.21: 'Arabia and all the princes of Kedar traded with you in lambs, rams, and goats' — the Arabian peninsula as the source of livestock for Tyre's markets. Kedar (H6938, the major confederation of north Arabian tribes mentioned in Assyrian records as Qidri) was the dominant pastoral power of the north Arabian steppe. The lambs and sheep supplied both wool/leather for Tyre's textile trade and animals for sacrificial use throughout the region's temples and festivals.
27.22: 'The merchants of Sheba and Raamah traded with you; they traded in the finest of all spices, all kinds of precious stones, and gold' — Sheba (southwestern Arabia, possibly modern Yemen) and Raamah (unidentified, possibly in the same region) as the luxury goods suppliers from the Arabian Peninsula. The trade route from southern Arabia (the 'Incense Route') brought frankincense, myrrh, spices, and gold from Arabia and East Africa to Mediterranean ports via Tyre. This is the same commercial network referenced when the Queen of Sheba visits Solomon (1 Kgs 10:1-10).
27.23: 'Haran, Canneh, Eden, the traders of Sheba, Asshur, and Chilmad were your merchants' — the northern Mesopotamian trade corridor: Haran (the major caravan city in northern Mesopotamia, also Abraham's stopping point, Gen 11:31), Canneh (possibly Calneh/Nippur in central Mesopotamia), Eden (possibly the bit-adini Aramean kingdom in the middle Euphrates region, known from Assyrian records). Asshur (Assyria) as a commercial partner even after Assyria's political fall — the infrastructure of Assyrian commerce survived the empire's collapse. This geographic sweep (from Spain/Tarshish in the west to Arabia/Sheba in the south to Mesopotamia/Asshur in the east) demonstrates Tyre's role as the commercial hub of the ancient world.
27.24: 'These traded with you in choice garments, in blue and embroidered cloth, in chests of fine clothing bound with cords and made of cedar' — the luxury textile trade in which Tyre both participated and set standards. 'Blue and embroidered cloth' (tekheleth and rikmah) — the same blue-violet dye and embroidered fabric used in the tabernacle furnishings (Exod 26:1,31) points to the shared material culture of the Levantine luxury textile industry.
27.25: 'The ships of Tarshish carried your merchandise; you were filled and made very glorious in the heart of the seas' — the 'ships of Tarshish' (oniyot Tarshish) were the largest class of Mediterranean merchant vessels, capable of the long ocean voyage to Spain. They are attested in multiple ancient sources as Phoenician/Tyrian ships. Isaiah 23:1-14 is entirely devoted to the mourning of the ships of Tarshish at Tyre's fall. The phrase 'filled and made very glorious' (wattikbedi me'od belbab yamim) captures the paradox of the allegory: the ship's wealth was also its fatal heaviness when the storm came.
27.26: 'Your rowers have brought you out into the great waters; the east wind has wrecked you in the heart of the seas' — the east wind (ruach qadim, the sirocco/khamsin) coming from the Babylonian east. The nautical reversal: the same ships that carried Tyre's merchandise to the world are now wrecked. The 'heart of the seas' (lev yamim) is where Tyre sat in commercial glory (v.4, 25) and now where it is wrecked — the same location transforms from the center of wealth to the place of destruction.
27.27: 'Your wealth, your merchandise, your goods, your mariners and pilots, your caulkers and dealers, all your men of war within you, and all your company that is in your midst will sink into the heart of the seas on the day of your fall' — the comprehensive list of everything that constituted Tyre's identity: commercial goods, the people who carried them (mariners, pilots, caulkers, traders), the military forces that defended them, all going down together. The catalog of Tyre's greatness (vv.3-25) becomes the catalog of total loss.
27.28: 'At the sound of the cry of your pilots the countryside shakes' — the sound that once represented Tyre's commercial authority (the pilots giving orders) now represents their distress cry. The shaking countryside (migrash, H4054, open land/suburbs) reverting to shock at the maritime world's collapse. The sound that structured the commerce of the ancient Mediterranean becomes the sound of lamentation.
27.29: 'All who handle the oar — the mariners and all the pilots of the sea — will come down from their ships and stand on dry land' — the reaction of the maritime world to Tyre's fall: sailors abandoning their ships, the professional mariners paralyzed with grief. The standing on dry land is a departure from their element — sailors who live at sea coming ashore in shock. The universality ('all who handle the oar') captures the scope of the commercial world's connection to Tyre.
27.30: 'They will raise their voices over you and cry out bitterly; they will throw dust on their heads and roll in ashes' — the formal mourning gestures of the ancient Near East: loud crying (za'aq), dust-throwing on the head, rolling in ashes. These are the same mourning gestures used for personal bereavement and for the fall of cities; the commercial partners of Tyre mourn its loss as a personal bereavement because their own livelihood depended on the Tyrian commercial network.
27.31: 'They will shave their heads completely for you and put on sackcloth; they will weep for you with bitter grief and bitter mourning' — the escalating mourning: shaved heads, sackcloth garments, bitter weeping. The formality of the mourning protocol indicates that the commercial partners' grief is genuine — not theatrical — because their economic existence was bound to Tyre. The fall of the commercial hub was literally catastrophic for those in its orbit.
27.32: 'Who was ever destroyed like Tyre, like one silent in the midst of the sea?' — the uniqueness of Tyre's fall: there was no precedent. 'Silent in the midst of the sea' (kedomah betoch hayam) — the noise of Tyre's commercial activity (songs, harps, v.26:13; merchant cries, ship sounds) is replaced by silence. The sea that once rang with Tyre's commerce becomes Tyre's silent grave.
27.33: 'When your merchandise went out over the seas, you satisfied many peoples; with your great wealth and goods you enriched the kings of the earth' — the tribute to Tyre's genuine commercial contribution: the city that satisfied (save'at) many peoples and enriched kings was not merely predatory but genuinely productive. The commercial system Tyre operated fed people, enriched regions, distributed goods. This acknowledgment of genuine benefit makes the fall more poignant and establishes that what is being mourned is a real civilizational achievement.
27.34: 'Now you are wrecked by the seas, in the depths of the waters; your merchandise and all your company have sunk with you' — the completed destruction: the past tense of fulfillment. The entire commercial edifice (merchandise, company, sailors, goods) has gone down together. The depths of the water complete the image begun with Tyre 'in the heart of the seas' — the center of the world becomes the bottom of the world.
27.35: 'All the inhabitants of the coastlands are appalled at you; their kings shudder with horror and their faces are troubled' — the reaction of the maritime political world to Tyre's fall. 'Appalled' (shamemu, H8074) and 'shudder with horror' (sa'ar, H8175) are the specific reactions to theophanic or catastrophic events — normally reserved for the response to YHWH's direct intervention. The fall of Tyre produces reactions appropriate to a divine act, because it is a divine act.
27.36: 'The merchants among the peoples hiss at you; you have come to a horrible end and will be no more forever' — the hissing (sharaq, H8319, whistle/hiss) is a conventional response to destroyed cities in the OT prophetic literature (Jer 19:8; 49:17; 50:13 — all using sharaq for the shocked reaction to ruined cities). 'Will be no more forever' (ayin ad olam) — the permanent dissolution of Tyre as a distinct commercial civilization closes the dirge.

28.1: The word-event formula introduces two closely related but distinct oracles: against the 'prince' (nasi) of Tyre (vv.1-10) and against the 'king' (melek) of Tyre (vv.11-19). The distinction between nasi and melek may reflect different aspects of the ruler's identity (his political vs. cosmic/mythological role) or may address different figures (the ruling king and an idealized/mythological Tyrian ruler).
28.2: 'Say to the prince of Tyre: Because your heart is proud and you have said, I am a god; I sit in the seat of gods in the heart of the sea — but you are a man, and not a god' — Ithobaal III (c.591-573 BCE), the reigning Tyrian king during Nebuchadnezzar's siege, and the Tyrian royal ideology of divine or semi-divine status. Phoenician kings claimed the patronage and protection of Baal, Melqart (the Tyrian deity), and El; the claim to 'sit in the seat of gods' reflects this ideology. The 'seat of gods' (moshav Elohim) in the heart of the sea: Tyre's island location was interpreted as a quasi-divine dwelling — the sea as the realm of the divine.
28.3: 'Behold, you are wiser than Daniel — no secret is hidden from you' — the reference to Daniel (Dan'el) is likely the same ancient figure as in 14:14: the legendary righteous and wise judge known from Ugaritic literature (the Aqhat epic, 14th-13th century BCE), where Dan'el (da-ni-il) is a righteous king renowned for judging the cause of the widow and orphan. The sarcastic comparison is rhetorically effective: the prince claims wisdom surpassing even the legendary Dan'el, but this very pride of wisdom is his indictment.
28.4: 'By your wisdom and understanding you have gained wealth for yourself and accumulated gold and silver in your treasuries' — the Tyrian prince's genuine commercial wisdom is acknowledged: he did achieve extraordinary wealth through real skill. This acknowledgment makes the indictment more pointed: the pride is not delusional but based on real achievement. The conversion of genuine skill into divine self-claim is the theological problem — what begins as competence becomes arrogance when it displaces recognition of the Creator.
28.5: 'By your great wisdom and in your trading you have increased your wealth, and your heart has become proud because of your wealth' — the spiritual mechanism of the fall: wisdom → trading → wealth → pride (gevah lev, proud heart). The Proverbs tradition consistently warns against the proud heart that results from prosperity (Prov 16:18; 18:12). The Tyrian prince's case is the paradigmatic example of this spiritual trajectory: legitimate commercial success generates illegitimate divine self-claim.
28.6: 'Therefore thus says the Lord GOD: Because you have set your heart as the heart of a god' — the precise charge: not merely that he claimed divine status rhetorically, but that he mentally and functionally placed himself in the category of deity. The 'heart as a god's heart' (lev levav Elohim) is the interior spiritual movement that precedes the outward claim. YHWH's judgment is addressed to the interior disposition, not merely the verbal claim.
28.7: 'Behold, I will bring strangers against you, the most ruthless of the nations' — the 'most ruthless of the nations' (araritzei goyim) is Ezekiel's recurring description of Babylon (26:7; 30:11; 31:12; 32:12). The Babylonian army as the instrument of YHWH's judgment against Tyrian hubris: the city that claimed divine status will be subjected to the most violent of human military powers. 'They will draw their swords against your beauty and wisdom' — the wisdom and beauty that constituted the divine claim will be the specific target of the sword.
28.8: 'They will thrust you down into the Pit, and you will die the death of the slain in the heart of the seas' — the throne in the 'seat of the gods in the heart of the sea' (v.2) becomes the grave in the 'heart of the seas.' The spatial inversion is complete: the divine dwelling becomes the place of death. 'The Pit' (shachat, H7845, pit/grave/corruption) is the underworld; dying in the sea rather than in the Pit is a different kind of death — without burial, without honor, lost in the element that was the prince's source of pride.
28.9: 'Will you still say, I am a god, before the one who kills you? But you will be a mere man in the hand of the one who wounds you' — the moment of the claim's falsification: death itself proves that the divine claim was false. The contrast between claiming to be a god (Elohim) and being 'a mere man' (adam) in the hand of one who wounds you is the rhetorical climax of the first oracle. Death is the universal human experience that destroys all pretensions to divinity.
28.10: 'You will die the death of the uncircumcised by the hand of foreigners, for I have spoken' — the shame of dying 'the death of the uncircumcised' (mot arelim): in Phoenician culture, circumcision was practiced (Herodotus 2.104 notes that Phoenicians practiced circumcision along with Egyptians and Ethiopians). To die 'the death of the uncircumcised' may mean dying without the honor of proper ritual and burial, or dying as those who are outside the covenant community — the ultimate social and cultic humiliation for one who claimed divine status.
28.11: The second oracle: against the 'king' (melek) of Tyre, using cosmic-mythological imagery unprecedented in the OT prophetic literature. The shift from nasi to melek may signal a shift in focus: from the present historical ruler to the idealized/primordial figure behind Tyrian royal ideology. The oracle draws on motifs from the broader ancient Near Eastern mythological tradition while subverting their claims.
28.12: 'You were the seal of perfection, full of wisdom and perfect in beauty' — tochnit (H8508, pattern/plan/seal) of perfection: the figure who is the perfect exemplar of what beauty and wisdom should be. The phrase 'full of wisdom and perfect in beauty' combines the two qualities the prince-oracle addressed — they have cosmic proportions in the king-oracle. The king of Tyre as the cosmic embodiment of the perfection that the creation intended.
28.13: 'You were in Eden, the garden of God; every precious stone adorned you' — the explicit Eden location: the king of Tyre is placed in Gan Eden (hagan-Eden, the garden of God/Eden). The list of nine precious stones (ruby, topaz, diamond/crystal, beryl, onyx, jasper, sapphire, turquoise, emerald) plus gold and the crafted settings corresponds to (and partially overlaps with) the high priest's breastplate stones (Exod 28:17-20). 'On the day you were created' (beyom hibbar'echa) — this is a created being, not eternal: the king of Tyre/the figure behind him was created, placed in Eden, adorned with precious stones.
28.14: 'You were the anointed guardian cherub (cherub mimshach hasokek); I had appointed you. You were on the holy mountain of God; you walked among the fiery stones' — the most theologically dense verse in the oracle. Three identifications: (1) anointed guardian cherub — a cherub given covenant anointing, placed as the guardian/covering cherub (cf. the cherubim who guard Eden in Gen 3:24); (2) on the holy mountain of God — the cosmic mountain of divine assembly (cf. Ps 48:2; Isa 14:13); (3) walking among fiery stones (beevenei-esh hithhalakta) — the divine throne-room phenomena of ch 1 (fiery stones in the vicinity of the chariot-throne). Whether this describes the historical Tyrian king in cosmic-mythological terms or an angelic being whose corruption underlies Tyre's spiritual reality has generated extensive theological discussion.
28.15: 'You were blameless in your ways from the day you were created until iniquity was found in you' — the perfect creation narrative: created without moral flaw, the iniquity found (passive: 'was found') as something that emerged from within. The phrase parallels both the pre-fall Adam (created good) and the Serpent's corrupting influence in the garden. The transition from blameless to iniquitous is the core of the oracle and mirrors the transition in Adam/Eve narratives.
28.16: 'Through the abundance of your trade you were filled inwardly with violence, and you sinned; so I cast you out as profane from the mountain of God' — the specific mechanism of the fall: commerce generating violence. rechullatechu (your trade/trafficking, H7404) as the source of the interior violence (chamas, H2555). The commercial activity that was Tyre's pride became the spiritual corrupting force. 'I cast you out as profane' (chalal, H2490, defiled/profaned) from the mountain of God — the expulsion from the divine mountain uses the language of ritual defilement: the sacred space cannot tolerate the profaned presence.
28.17: 'Your heart was proud because of your beauty; you corrupted your wisdom for the sake of your splendor. I have cast you to the ground; I have exposed you before kings to feast their eyes on you' — the pride-fall pattern: beauty → pride → corrupted wisdom → public humiliation before kings. The 'feast their eyes on you' (ra'o becha) is the public spectacle of the humiliated proud: the kings of the earth watch the exposure of the one who claimed to be above them.
28.18: 'By the abundance of your iniquities and the wrongdoing of your trade you defiled your sanctuaries; therefore I have brought fire from the midst of you' — the fire from within: not external destruction but internal combustion. The 'sanctuaries' (miqddashecha) that were defiled — the sacred spaces of Tyre, including the famous temple of Melqart described by Herodotus (2.44). 'It has consumed you' — Tyre consumed by its own internal fire, the violence that trade generated eventually destroying the trader.
28.19: 'All who knew you among the peoples are appalled at you; you have become a horror and shall be no more forever' — the same closing formula as the ch 27 dirge (27:36): appalled (shamemu) at the one who was the seal of perfection; horror (ballahot); no more forever. The king-oracle closes with the same language of permanent dissolution as the ship-allegory, tying the two chapters together in a single extended meditation on Tyrian greatness and its judgment.
28.20: A brief oracle transition — the word-event formula marks the shift to the Sidon oracle. Sidon was the senior Phoenician city (Tyre was traditionally its daughter-colony) and was politically and commercially linked to Tyre throughout the period. The oracle against Sidon is much shorter than the Tyre oracles, reflecting Sidon's lesser commercial and cultic significance in Ezekiel's period.
28.21: 'Son of man, set your face against Sidon and prophesy against it' — Sidon (H6721, possibly connected to 'fishing'), the city 40 km north of Tyre on the Phoenician coast. The sim panim el (set-face) formula is the standard opening for the OAN oracles (cf. 6:2; 13:17; 25:2; 28:2; 29:2). Sidon was involved in the anti-Babylonian coalition referenced in Jer 27:3 (Sidon sent envoys to Jerusalem along with Edom, Moab, Ammon, and Tyre to coordinate resistance to Babylon).
28.22: 'Thus says the Lord GOD: Behold, I am against you, Sidon; I will be glorified in your midst. They will know that I am the LORD when I execute judgments on her and show myself holy in her' — the Sidon oracle is primarily a recognition-formula oracle. The key terms: nikhbadti ('I will be glorified/honored') and weniqdashti ('I will show myself holy') — YHWH's self-disclosure through Sidon's judgment. The oracle against Sidon is less detailed than those against Tyre because its primary purpose is to complete the Phoenician city-state section of the OAN before the Egyptian section begins.
28.23: 'I will send pestilence into her and blood into her streets; the slain will fall in her midst by the sword that is against her on every side; then they will know that I am the LORD' — pestilence (dever) and sword as the standard dual instruments of urban judgment. The recognition formula closes the brief oracle. Sidon's historical defeat at the hands of Artaxerxes III in 345 BCE (the city was burned and 40,000 inhabitants reportedly died) is sometimes cited as a fulfillment, though the timeframe differs significantly from Ezekiel's context.
28.24: 'There will no longer be a painful brier or a piercing thorn from any of the surrounding peoples who showed contempt for Israel' — the transition from judgment-oracle to restoration-promise for Israel. The 'brier and thorn' (sillon mam'ir and qotz mak'ov) language echoes Num 33:55 (the Canaanites who remain will be 'pricks in your eyes and thorns in your sides'). The judgment of Tyre, Sidon, and the other neighboring peoples eliminates the persistent hostile pressure on Israel from its commercial and political neighbors.
28.25: 'Thus says the Lord GOD: When I gather the house of Israel from the peoples among whom they have been scattered, and show myself holy in them in the sight of the nations, then they will dwell in their own land that I gave to my servant Jacob' — the restoration promise that closes the entire OAN section (chs 25-28): the gathering of scattered Israel, YHWH's sanctification displayed before the nations, and the return to the Jacobite inheritance. The 'servant Jacob' (avdi ya'aqov) is the patriarchal ground of the land promise — the same promise that undergirds the restoration promises of chs 34-37.
28.26: 'They will dwell in it safely and build houses and plant vineyards. They will live with confidence when I have executed judgments on all their neighbors who have shown contempt for them; then they will know that I am the LORD their God' — the picture of the restored Israel: security (betach, H983, in safety), building houses, planting vineyards — the standard covenant-blessing formula for settled abundance (Isa 65:21-22; Amos 9:14). The causal link: the security of restored Israel is contingent on the judgment of the hostile neighbors. The recognition formula here is 'I am the LORD their God' — with the possessive: after all the OAN oracles in which YHWH revealed himself to foreign nations through their judgments, he reveals himself to Israel as their specific covenant Lord.

23.1: Chapter 23 returns to the two-sisters allegory. Unlike the ch 16 foundling allegory that focused on Jerusalem alone, ch 23 presents both kingdoms — Israel (northern, Samaria) and Judah (southern, Jerusalem) — as co-wives of YHWH whose careers of political prostitution are traced in parallel. The allegory is more explicitly political than ch 16: the "lovers" are identified by national name rather than left as general partners.
23.2: Two women, daughters of one mother: Both Samaria and Jerusalem share the same covenantal origin — both are daughters of the original covenant people formed at Sinai. The shared mother grounds the comparison: these are not different national identities but two branches of the same covenant community. The parallel histories of apostasy that follow implicate the unified origin in the shared guilt.
23.3: Egypt as origin of the prostitution: The sexual activity began in Egypt — "their breasts were squeezed there and their virgin nipples fondled." This startling claim grounds the political prostitution in the Exodus era: Israel's fundamental identity was formed in Egypt, and Egypt was the first arena of corrupt political-religious compromise. Historically, the pre-Sinai period includes the Israelites' worship of Egyptian gods (Josh 24:14; Ezek 20:7-8) and Solomon's Egyptian marriages (1 Kgs 3:1; 11:1-8).
23.4: The sisters' names: Oholah ('her tent/tabernacle') and Oholibah ('my tent is in her'). The names are etymologically significant: Samaria (Oholah) has her own tent — a sanctuary that belongs to herself, not to YHWH (the Bethel/Dan bull sanctuaries of Jeroboam I, 1 Kgs 12:28-29). Jerusalem (Oholibah) has YHWH's tent within her — the temple. The names encode the theological verdict: Samaria had an unauthorized cult from her founding; Jerusalem had a legitimate cult that she betrayed.
23.5: Oholah (Samaria) and Assyria: The northern kingdom's entanglement with Assyria began with Menahem's tribute to Tiglath-Pileser III (745-727 BCE) — 1 Kings 15:19-20: a thousand talents of silver to secure Assyrian backing for his throne. Pekah's anti-Assyrian coalition and Hosea's submission followed. The "Assyrians as lovers" metaphor in Ezekiel captures the political reality: Israel bought Assyrian favor with tribute, becoming economically and politically dependent on the empire that would eventually destroy it.
23.6: Assyrian warriors in their military glory: Ezekiel's description of the Assyrian warriors — clothed in purple, governors and commanders, horsemen, desirable young men — reflects accurate knowledge of Assyrian military display. Neo-Assyrian reliefs (the Nineveh palace reliefs now in the British Museum) show exactly this: officials in purple robes, cavalry units with ornamental gear, hierarchical military organization. The "desirable" quality of the Assyrian military machine was its disciplined power, which smaller states both feared and sought to harness through alliance.
23.7: Oholah gave herself to "the choice of all the Assyrians" — the elite of the empire. The political alliances Samaria made were with the highest levels of Assyrian power, not minor border officials. Kol-gilulei Assyria ('all the idols of Assyria') — the political prostitution was inseparable from the religious: accepting the Assyrian political order meant accepting the Assyrian gods (2 Kgs 16:10-16; Ahaz adopted the Damascene altar after meeting Tiglath-Pileser).
23.8: The Egyptian "prostitution" continued: Oholah (Samaria) never abandoned the Egypt-rooted apostasy from her beginnings. The northern kingdom maintained religious forms that had Egyptian associations (bull-calf iconography at Bethel and Dan echoes the Egyptian Apis bull and Canaanite El-bull imagery). The continued Egypt-prostitution alongside the Assyrian alliance represents the layering of successive apostasies without abandonment of earlier ones.
23.9: Handed over to her Assyrian lovers: The historical event: the Assyrian destruction of Samaria in 722 BCE under Sargon II, following the siege begun by Shalmaneser V. 2 Kings 17:3-6 records that Hoshea, king of Israel, submitted to Shalmaneser but then sent messengers to Egypt and withheld tribute — the same Egypt-over-Assyria shift that Ezekiel 23:7-8 describes. The people were deported to Halah, Habor, the Gozan River, and the cities of the Medes — scattered across the Assyrian empire.
23.10: The execution of Oholah: Gallu ba — those who executed her were her own lovers, the Assyrians. The typical Assyrian punishment for a rebellious vassal included stripping (seizure of wealth), public humiliation, and execution of the leadership. Veyhi shem le'nashim ('she became a name/byword among women'): the northern kingdom's fate became the cautionary example that prophets like Hosea used. That Ezekiel's Judean audience knew this example and still repeated it is the charge of v.11.
23.11: "Her sister Oholibah saw this... and was more corrupt": The logic of the comparison is devastating: Jerusalem witnessed Samaria's destruction by Assyria and still repeated the same pattern — and exceeded it. Historically, the fall of the northern kingdom in 722 BCE was within living memory of Ezekiel's generation (a century). The prophets Micah and Isaiah used the fall of Samaria as a warning to Judah in exactly this way (Mic 1:6-7; Isa 28:1-4).
23.12: Oholibah lusted after Assyria too: Judah's Assyrian entanglements: Ahaz's appeal to Tiglath-Pileser III against the Aram-Ephraim coalition (2 Kgs 16:7-9) — paying tribute from the temple treasury. Ahaz then visited Damascus to meet Tiglath-Pileser and adopted an Assyrian altar design for the Jerusalem temple (2 Kgs 16:10-16). Isaiah condemned this as reliance on human power over YHWH (Isa 7-8; 30-31). The governors and commanders, the horsemen — the same imagery as v.6.
23.13: YHWH's declaration: Va'ereh ki nit'ame shetayyhem ('I saw that both were defiled'). The divine observation confirms the parallel guilt. Despite witnessing Oholah's destruction, Oholibah followed the same pattern — the witness-experience that should have produced repentance instead stimulated imitation. The moral psychology here is realistic: it is easier to continue familiar patterns than to be changed by observing another's judgment, especially when the judgment seems remote.
23.14: The Babylonian murals: Vatosif el-ta'avoteha vatere anshei mequtteim al-haqir tzalmei Kasdim chaquqim basheshar ('she increased her prostitution and she saw men carved on the wall, images of Chaldeans carved in vermilion/red ochre'). This is a remarkable historical detail. Neo-Babylonian palace decoration featured large-scale relief carvings and painted murals of military figures, courtiers, and processions — visible to any visitor to Babylon's administrative buildings. Ezekiel and his fellow exiles had firsthand exposure to these murals in Babylon.
23.15: Belted turbans and flowing robes: The detailed description of the Babylonian figures — chaluzei meznachim al-motnehem yeru'ot tvolet al-rasheihem ('girded with belts at their waists, with flowing turbans on their heads') — matches Neo-Babylonian artistic conventions. Cylinder seals and relief carvings from the 6th century BCE show Babylonian officials in exactly this attire: wrapped sashes, tall turbans or tiered headdresses, long robes. The visual specificity suggests Ezekiel (or his source) had direct knowledge of Babylonian visual culture, consistent with his exile location.
23.16: Desire at first sight: Vatit'aveh alehem lema'ereh eineiha ('she lusted after them at the sight of her eyes'): the visual stimulus triggering the political-religious desire. The sending of messengers (vatishlach malakhim alehem Kasdimah) = the diplomatic overtures from Jerusalem to Babylon. Historical referent: Hezekiah's hospitality to the Babylonian envoys of Merodach-Baladan (2 Kgs 20:12-15; Isa 39:1-4) — an early Babylonian alliance that Isaiah condemned as the first step toward the ultimate Babylonian captivity.
23.17: The Babylonians coming to her bed: The Chaldean-lust allegory covers multiple phases of Judah-Babylonian relations: the Hezekiah-Merodach-Baladan contact (705 BCE), Manasseh's submission to Babylon (2 Chr 33:11), the complex vassalage of Jehoiakim and Zedekiah under Nebuchadnezzar (605-587 BCE). Each phase is a successive "coming to bed." Vatitma bam ('she was defiled by them'): the defilement comes precisely through the alliances that were supposed to provide security.
23.18: The alienation of YHWH: Vatiqal nafshi me'aleiha ('my soul recoiled from her'): YHWH's emotional withdrawal from Jerusalem mirrors the estrangement in 16:15 and the departure of the glory in ch 10-11. The word qal nafshi (my soul was alienated/repulsed) uses the same root as qalas (to be light, contemptible). As Oholah had been handed over to Assyria, so Oholibah will be handed over to those from whom she has now turned away.
23.19: Increased prostitution recalling Egyptian youth: Oholibah's third phase: returning to Egypt-desire after the Babylonian phase. This mirrors Zedekiah's pro-Egyptian policy — reaching out to Egypt for help against Babylon (2 Kgs 24:20; Jer 37:5-8). The "days of her youth in Egypt" refers to the same Egyptian-origin apostasy introduced in v.3. The cycle returns to its beginning, but with greater intensity. Each successive alliance does not replace but compounds the previous ones.
23.20: Egypt's explicit description: Asher basar chamorim besaram ('whose flesh was like donkey flesh'): the most sexually explicit line in Ezekiel, equating Egypt's sexual attraction to bestiality. The political commentary is buried in the biology: Egypt's appeal was raw, animal power with no covenantal quality — pure imperial bulk without relational loyalty. Egypt's military as a "great flow of water" (zirmat susim zimratam) was the fantasy of military might that Israel imagined would protect them but never did (Isa 30:7: "Egypt's help is worthless and empty").
23.21: The return to Egyptian desire is described as 'reviving the lewdness of your youth' — the circle of apostasy is complete. Ezekiel frames Israel's history as an extended return to the starting point: everything that happened between Sinai and Zedekiah was a variation on the original failure to abandon Egyptian religious-political habits. The final appeal to Egypt is not a new departure but a regression to the oldest pattern.
23.22: The lovers turned enemies: The theological principle underlying the judgment: the lovers (alliances) whom Oholibah used will be turned against her. YHWH brings up her former allies (mesanim alayich) as the instruments of judgment. The former ally becomes the instrument of divine judgment: Babylon, which Jerusalem sought as a lover, returns as her executioner. This is the inner logic of covenant-idolatry — the power trusted in place of YHWH becomes the mechanism of punishment.
23.23: The Babylonian coalition: Pqod uShoa uQoa — possibly specific peoples or districts of the Babylonian empire. Kol-beney Ashshur (all the sons of Assyria): Assyrian military units incorporated into the Babylonian imperial force. The multinational character of the Babylonian army (Jer 52:28-30 mentions various national components) made it the representative instrument of international judgment — 'all the nations' against Jerusalem.
23.24: Battle equipment and judicial execution: Ve'ata alayich choshen reken ('they will come against you with weapons, chariots, wagons'): the full military equipment of the Neo-Babylonian army. The juridical element — veneshpatu otach kemishpatam ('they will judge you according to their judgments') — is crucial: the Babylonians' punishment of Jerusalem is simultaneously YHWH's judicial verdict executed through human agents. The pagan army becomes YHWH's court officers.
23.25: Divine jealousy and the removal of nose and ears: Vehishteta ki ani YHWH ('you will know that I am YHWH'). The mutilation — vesaru appeikh ve'oznayich ('they will take your nose and your ears') — reflects actual ancient Near Eastern punishments for adultery. Middle Assyrian Laws (tablet A, §4-5, §15) prescribed nose-cutting for a woman caught in adultery. The judicial punishment fits the allegorical crime. The removal of the ornaments (16:11-13) was the first stripping; the removal of nose and ears is the ultimate deforming punishment.
23.26: The stripping of garments and jewelry echoes the reversal of the bridal adornment in 16:39. Everything YHWH gave in the betrothal-lavishing is removed in the punishment-stripping. The divine gifts — embroidered cloth, jewelry, ornaments — are taken away by the very forces Jerusalem courted in place of YHWH. The lovers strip the bride who betrayed the husband.
23.27: The end of the prostitution and the Egyptian connection: Vehishbatti zimmateich mimennech ('I will put an end to your lewdness from you'). The purpose of the judgment is explicitly therapeutic/remedial: the punishment ends the behavior. Velo tissi einayich alehem veMitzrayim lo tizkerevat od ('and you will not lift your eyes to them, and Egypt you will remember no more'): the Egyptian desire that has persisted since the beginning (v.3) will finally end. The judgment accomplishes what repentance should have achieved voluntarily.
23.28: The principle of judgment through the rejected: YHWH delivers Jerusalem into the hand of those she hates — the enemies created by the withdrawal of the alliances. Historical reality: when Zedekiah's rebellion against Babylon failed, Egypt withdrew rather than fight to the finish (Jer 37:7-8). The "lovers" — Babylonian and Egyptian — both became hostile; the two great powers Jerusalem tried to play against each other both turned against her.
23.29: The full stripping: Ve'asu otach be'sinah ('they will deal with you in hatred'): the former lovers act with active malice. Velaqachu kol yegia'ech ('they will take all your earnings/property'): the stripping is also an economic stripping — the accumulated wealth of Jerusalem becomes war plunder. The nakedness and exposure follow the same logic as 16:37-41: the very partners in the apostasy execute the punishment, which is poetically just.
23.30: The summary verdict: Asher asit etkeh ('these things are done to you'). The explicit causal chain: Jerusalem did these things; therefore these things happen to her. The moral coherence of the judgment is insisted upon: this is not random catastrophe but the proportionate consequence of documented infidelity. The nations she went to (asher zagit achar ha-goyim) are the nations through whom she is judged.
23.31: The cup passed from Oholah to Oholibah: Bederekh achoteikh halacht ('you walked in the way of your sister'): the imitation principle. The cup of Samaria — the Assyrian destruction — was Oholibah's warning and example; instead of avoiding it, Oholibah drank the same cup. The cup of judgment (kos achoteikh) passes between the sisters. The cup-of-wrath imagery (Isa 51:17-22; Jer 25:15-29; Hab 2:16) is a common prophetic trope for divine judgment.
23.32: The cup of Samaria: deep and wide: The cup's dimensions — deep and wide — indicate comprehensiveness of judgment. Tihyeh litsechok ulelag ('it will hold much laughter and mockery'): the cup provokes mockery from observers who watch the judgment unfold. The surrounding nations who were sympathetic to Samaria's claims will watch Jerusalem's fall with the same contemptuous mockery (Lam 1:7; 2:15-16).
23.33: Intoxication of devastation: Shikaron vetsuqah ('drunkenness and desolation'): the double effect of the cup — drunken incapacity and devastation. The combination captures the complete disorientation and ruin of the city that must drink the full cup of judgment. The cup of Samaria's sister is identical in effect though delivered through a different imperial power.
23.34: The full consumption of the cup — tishetihu vetimtzehu ('you will drink it and drain it') — and the gnawing of the potsherds: the complete consumption and even the residue scraped from the vessel. Nothing of the judgment is spared; the cup is emptied completely. The tearing of one's own breasts (veshodayich tigenami) — extreme grief expressed through self-harm — is the ultimate expression of the cup's effect.
23.35: The verdict: "You have forgotten me": The theological summary: ya'an asher shakachti oti ('because you forgot me'). The root failure is forgetting YHWH — the same diagnosis as 16:22. Vetishlichi oti acharei gavech ('and cast me behind your back'): the body-language metaphor of dismissal — turning one's back on YHWH. The judgment follows as the direct consequence: vegam at se'i zimmateich vetaz nuteich ('you yourself bear your lewdness and your prostitution').
23.36: YHWH calls Ezekiel to judge both sisters jointly (hatishpot et-Oholah ve'et-Oholibah): the combined indictment of Israel and Judah. The shared guilt is named: the blood on their hands and the idols in their houses. The joint accusation is a theological statement: despite the separate histories and separate divine acts of judgment (Assyria destroyed Samaria; Babylon destroyed Jerusalem), both kingdoms share equally in the guilt of covenant apostasy.
23.37: Child sacrifice specified: Both sisters committed adultery with idols and sacrificed their children born to YHWH. The child sacrifice charge from 16:20-21 is repeated here for the northern kingdom as well: this was not exclusively a southern sin. The archaeological evidence from the Tophet at Carthage (a Phoenician/Canaanite site) confirms child sacrifice practices across the wider Canaanite cultural sphere, suggesting the Israelite apostasy adopted real regional practices.
23.38: Temple defilement and Sabbath profanation: The two-fold defilement — the sanctuary defiled (timme'u et-miqdashi) and the Sabbaths profaned (ve'et-Shabbetotai chillelu) — on the same day as child sacrifice. The juxtaposition is horrifying: bringing blood-stained hands directly into the sanctuary for worship. This is the exact situation Isaiah condemns in Isaiah 1:10-15: 'I cannot bear your evil assemblies... stop bringing meaningless offerings; your incense is detestable to me... when you spread out your hands in prayer, I hide my eyes from you; even when you offer many prayers, I am not listening. Your hands are full of blood.'
23.39: The desecration sequence: children killed in the morning, then the parents coming to the temple. The temporal sequence makes the horror explicit — the same day, the same hands, the same people moving between Molech and YHWH. The behavioral pattern that Jeremiah identified as 'entering this house' after doing the things YHWH hates (Jer 7:9-11) is here described in its most extreme form.
23.40: Sending for men from far away: The summons to the nations as lovers. Ve'af ki shelachten la'anashim ba'im memerchaq ('and even to men coming from afar you sent a messenger'): the diplomatic overtures to distant powers. The bathing, painting eyes (vatakahal einayich — kohl/eye makeup), and adornment for the visitor parallels the female preparation for a paramour — the diplomatic hospitality as sexual preparation. This is an accurate description of the diplomatic reception parties that characterized ancient Near Eastern statecraft.
23.41: The couch and the prepared table: Veyashavt al-mita kevuda ushulchan aruch lefaneiha ('you sat on an honorable couch with a table spread before it'): the formal diplomatic banquet setting. The incense and oil from YHWH's provision used in the diplomatic reception for foreign emissaries — the covenantal gifts redirected to impress potential political allies. The 'table' imagery anticipates the critique of 39:20 and 44:16 where YHWH's own table (the altar) is the proper context for these goods.
23.42: Noise of carefree crowd: The crowd with the women (qa-kol hamon shalevim bah) represents the diplomatic parties and the festive atmosphere of the court-prostitution. Uvasabba'im me'imidbar itam tzamidim el-yedeihen ('with drunkards brought from the desert, they put bracelets on their hands'): the reveling mixed crowd receiving jewelry — the bridal gifts (16:11-13) distributed to foreign visitors. The transformation of the sacred marriage gifts into diplomatic favors.
23.43: The divine rhetorical question: Ha'am lezi'nah titzneh hu — the exhausted and worn-out woman continuing to prostitute herself. The image is of a court that has exhausted itself through successive alliances yet continues to pursue more. The political-theological exhaustion of Jerusalem under its last kings — the rapid succession of alliances and betrayals under Jehoiakim and Zedekiah — is captured in the image of the worn-out prostitute still soliciting.
23.44: The lovers going in to the prostitute as one goes to a loose woman — the contempt of the nations for Jerusalem, which has made herself universally available. The comparison between the temple-prostitute (if reading zonah as cult prostitute) and Jerusalem is the point: Jerusalem has made herself like the very worst category of religious-sexual practice she was supposed to transcend.
23.45: Righteous men as judges: Ve'anashim tzaddiqim hemah yishpetu otam mishpat no'afot umishpat shofechot dam ('righteous men — they will judge them with the judgment of adulteresses and with the judgment of women who shed blood'): the legal category. The "righteous men" are the instruments of the judicial community exercising the covenant law. The punishment for adultery and child murder is named without specification — the law was clear (Lev 20:2, 10) and both crimes carried capital sentences.
23.46: Mob assembly: Ki koh amar Adonai YHWH ha'al aleichem qahal ('for thus says the Lord YHWH: bring up against them an assembly'): the qahal (assembly/mob) as executioner. The legal execution by stoning involved community participation; here the 'assembly' is the invading Babylonian forces serving as executors of the communal death penalty. The warfare is juridical in Ezekiel's framing: Babylon is the divine court's enforcement mechanism, not merely a conquering empire.
23.47: Stoning and sword together: the assembly executes the verdict with both the traditional communal (stoning) and military (sword) methods. The killing of sons and daughters (beneihem uvnoteihem yaharegu) — the covenant-curse reversal: the children who were sacrificed (v.37) are now joined in death by those who survive the initial assault. The houses burned: the complete destruction of property accompanying the judicial execution.
23.48: The instructive purpose of judgment: Lenashim mussar ('a warning to women') — the judgment of the two sisters is explicitly pedagogical. The surrounding nations who observe Jerusalem's punishment will receive the instruction that covenant apostasy leads to such ends. The public nature of the judgment is integral to its purpose: YHWH's judicial actions are transparent, comprehensible, and serve as moral education for all who witness them.
23.49: Bearing the guilt of idolatry: Venasatu zimmateichem aleyhen ('you will bear the guilt of your idol-worship upon yourselves'): individual and corporate accountability stated as the final principle. Vidatetem ki Ani Adonai YHWH: the recognition formula closes the extended double-allegory. The entire chapter builds toward this: the goal of the comprehensive judgment on both sisters is that they will know — finally, through the enacted consequences — that YHWH alone is God.

24.1: The precise date of the siege: Bayom ha'atem ba'shanah hateshi'it bachodesh ha'asiri be'asor lachodesh ('on this very day, in the ninth year, in the tenth month, on the tenth day of the month'): this date — 9th year, 10th month, 10th day — is independently confirmed in 2 Kings 25:1 and Jeremiah 52:4 as the date Nebuchadnezzar began the siege of Jerusalem (approximately January 588 BCE by modern reckoning). Ezekiel receives this oracle in Babylon on the same day the siege begins in Jerusalem — a demonstration of prophetic knowledge across geographic distance.
24.2: The command to record the date: Khtov lecha et-shem hayom et-etzem hayom hazeh ('write down for yourself the name of this day, this very day'): the unprecedented command to record the date anchors the prophetic word in specific historical time. The date-marking anticipates verification: when the exiles in Babylon later learn of the actual siege's beginning, they will be able to check Ezekiel's recorded date. The prophecy is not vague but date-stamped for historical accountability.
24.3: The allegory of the rusty pot: The cooking-pot allegory (dud) returns from 11:3 where the inhabitants of Jerusalem sarcastically said "this city is the pot and we are the meat." There, they imagined the pot as protective. Ezekiel here turns the imagery: the pot is rusty/corroded (the bloodguilt of the city), the fire of judgment cannot be quenched, and the contents will be consumed. The reversal of the populace's own comforting allegory is deliberate and devastating.
24.4: The choice pieces in the pot: Aspah neta'iha aleyha kol-netach tov yarekh vekhatef ('gather into it piece by piece — every good piece, the thigh and the shoulder'): the best cuts are placed in the pot first. The allegory may refer to the inhabitants who remained in Jerusalem after the first deportation (597 BCE), who considered themselves the "good pieces" chosen to remain (cf. 11:15 where they say "get far from YHWH; to us this land has been given"). In the allegory, they are indeed chosen — for cooking in the pot of judgment.
24.5: The piling of wood and the boiling: The thorough preparation of the fire — piling wood high under the pot, boiling the bones within it. The fire's intensity mirrors the intensity of the coming judgment. Ratch ratchachta ('boil thoroughly'): the doubled verb of thorough boiling — nothing will be left undercooked or partially judged. The completeness of the boiling is the completeness of the coming siege and destruction.
24.6: The city of blood and its rust: Ir haddamim (the bloody city) from 22:2; the rust/scum (chaluddah) — either rust or verdigris — represents the accumulated bloodguilt that has corroded the pot. Piece by piece being taken out (hotze netacheiha nefach nefach) without casting lots — unlike the normal meat division where lots determined portions, here there is no preference: all pieces are equally contaminated, equally subject to judgment. The absence of lot-casting signals the totalistic nature of the coming punishment.
24.7: Blood on bare rock: Ki dama betochah hayah ('for her blood is in her midst'): the blood of judicial murder poured on bare rock rather than covered with dust (as Job 16:18 and Gen 4:10 describe blood crying out from the ground). The explicit refusal to cover blood (lo shefachthu al-pnei ha'aretz lekhasoto afar) means the bloodguilt cannot be neutralized — it remains open, crying, unatoned. Leviticus 17:13 required that blood be covered with earth; Jerusalem's blood was left exposed, symbolizing either defiance of the Torah or murder too public to conceal.
24.8: Blood exposed to provoke wrath: Latit chamat liqom naqam ('to provoke wrath to take vengeance'): the open blood is described as YHWH's deliberate act — he put it on bare rock where it would remain visible. The blood that cries from the ground (Gen 4:10: Abel's blood to YHWH) here becomes the permanent visible accusation. The open blood of Jerusalem's injustice and murder sits permanently exposed, requiring divine response. No statute of limitations; no dust to cover it.
24.9: YHWH's direct declaration: Hoy ir haddamim ('woe, city of blood!'): the funeral-woe formula over the living city. Gam-ani agdil ha-medurah ('I also will make the pile great'): YHWH himself intensifies the fire. The judgment is not an unfortunate accident but an active divine decision to make the fire greater, the burning more complete. The divine agency in the Babylonian siege — YHWH enlarging the attacking army's fire — is the theological claim that makes the historical catastrophe meaningful.
24.10: Heaping on wood, kindling fire: The repeated emphasis on fuel and fire preparation. Hattem et-habassar veharkech hamarqa ('cook the meat and mix in the spices'): the thoroughly cooked meat with spices — the siege conditions that will "cook" Jerusalem's inhabitants. Veha'atzmot yicharru ('and the bones shall be burned'): the cooking extends to the bones — total consumption, nothing left over. Archaeological evidence of the Babylonian destruction of Jerusalem (586 BCE) confirms massive burning of buildings and destruction of the city's material fabric.
24.11: The empty pot on the coals: After the contents are consumed, the pot itself is placed on the coals empty — the purpose being to burn away the rust/filth (chaluddatah titom bamah, 'so that its uncleanness may be melted in it'). The purification of the pot after its contents are consumed. The logic: even after the people are removed through conquest and exile, the land itself requires a period of desolation (the empty-land Sabbath of Lev 26:34-35) to purify itself of the accumulated ritual and moral contamination.
24.12: The rust that will not come out: Rabba heli'ah ('she has wearied me with toil'): the rust/uncleanness of Jerusalem is so deeply embedded that even being placed empty on the fire cannot remove it. Ve'eiyah yetze mimmennah chaluddatah benur ('and her great rust will not come out with fire'): the extreme level of contamination that cannot be remediated by partial measures. The theological conclusion: only complete destruction and the new-creation work of YHWH (chs 36-37) will cleanse what the fire of judgment cannot purify.
24.13: The filth of lewdness: Betuma'ateh zimmah ('in your unclean lewdness'): the accumulated moral contamination. Ya'an tihartich velo tahert mituma'ateh ('because I tried to purify you and you were not purified of your uncleanness'): the iterative divine cleansing attempts that failed. YHWH tried to purify Jerusalem through the partial judgments (Assyrian siege of 701, Babylonian deportation of 597) without success. The final fire is not the first attempt but the last in a series of failed remedial measures.
24.14: The irreversibility of the judgment: Ani YHWH dibarti ba'ah ve'asiti ('I, YHWH, have spoken; it is coming and I will act'). The divine commitment is irrevocable: lo eftsa velo-achus velo-echanem ('I will not hold back, and I will not have pity, and I will not relent'). The triple negative of divine resolve mirrors the triple refusal of mercy from 8:18 and 9:10. The time for intercession and amendment has passed; the word has gone forth and cannot be recalled. Cf. Isaiah 55:11: 'my word... will not return to me empty.'
24.15: A new, devastating oracle. The transition from the pot allegory to the personal sign-act of the wife's death marks the shift from cosmic to intimate. This is the most personally costly sign-act Ezekiel performs — more than lying on his side, more than the hair burning, more than the siege mockup. This time the prophet's own household is the instrument of divine communication.
24.16: The death of Ezekiel's wife: Ben-adam hinneni lokeach mimmecha et-machmad einecha bemageifa ('son of man, I am about to take from you the desire of your eyes with a plague'). Machmad einecha ('the desire of your eyes') is the most intimate of Hebrew endearments — the person most precious, the one you look for. The announcement that YHWH will take this person "at a stroke" (bemageifa, by plague/sudden blow) and Ezekiel must not mourn is the most demanding sign-act command in the entire prophetic corpus.
24.17: The prohibition on mourning: He'aneq dom meitim avel lo-ta'aseh ('moan quietly, mourn for the dead — do not do it'): the inversion of normal mourning duty. Standard Israelite mourning included: loud lamentation (beki), removing shoes (ne'alecha asim be'raglecha), covering the beard (safam lo ta'ateh), and eating mourners' bread (lechem anashim lo-tochal). Ezekiel is commanded to violate all of these simultaneously. The mourning practices he must not perform were deeply embedded cultural obligations — neglecting them was an act of social ostracism under normal circumstances.
24.18: Ezekiel's obedience: Va'edabber el-ha'am baboqer vatamot ishti ba'erev ('I spoke to the people in the morning, and in the evening my wife died'): the juxtaposition is stark. Ezekiel prophesied in the morning; his wife died in the evening; in the morning he did not mourn as commanded. The narrative economy — no description of grief, no admission of sorrow — is not indicative of emotional numbness but of deliberate literary restraint that forces the reader to supply the imagination of what this cost.
24.19: The people's question: Halo tagid lanu mah-elleh lanu ki ata oseh ('will you not tell us what these things mean for us that you are doing?'): the sign-act works as intended — the people notice and ask. The prophetic sign-act tradition required observable behavior that provoked query (cf. 12:9; 21:7). Ezekiel's refusal to mourn his wife publicly is so countercultural that it demands explanation.
24.20: Ezekiel identifies the meaning: the word of YHWH came to him. The oracle that follows is the interpretation of the sign-act. Ezekiel's personal loss is a prophetic miniature of the people's imminent loss — the wife he loved as they loved the sanctuary. The sign-act makes the theological unbearable personal: you cannot grieve properly for what is coming.
24.21: The sanctuary as the people's "desire of the eyes": Machmad einechem ('the desire of your eyes'): the same phrase used for Ezekiel's wife (v.16) is now applied to the temple — Hineni mechalel et-miqdashi ('I am about to profane/defile my sanctuary'). The sanctuary as the most precious thing in Israel's existence — more than political independence, more than territorial integrity — is about to be taken. The people will be unable to mourn properly just as Ezekiel could not mourn his wife.
24.22: The same prohibition imposed on the people: Va'asitem ka'asher asiti ('and you will do as I have done'): the parallel is made explicit. The people will not cover their lips or eat mourners' bread — the communal disaster will be so overwhelming, so comprehensive, that normal grief rituals will be suspended. The psychological state described matches what we know of populations under total siege: numbness, inability to process, the overwhelming of ordinary grief-processing capacity.
24.23: The signs of normal mourning — turbans on heads, sandals on feet (the opposite of the normal removal) — because the disaster is too complete for mourning rituals to address. Uneqattam ba'avontechem ('and you will waste away/pine in your iniquities'): the survivors will be hollowed out from within by the weight of the recognized guilt. Venehemtem ish el-achiv ('and you will groan to one another'): private grief replacing public ritual — the sighing of Ezekiel at the beginning of the chapter (v.17: he'aneq dom) now extended to the whole people.
24.24: Ezekiel as the sign: Uvekhol-asher asah laken tihyu ('and in all that he did, so will you'): Ezekiel is explicitly the sign-figure — his life is the prophetic commentary on the people's life. His wife's death and his non-mourning become the template for the people's experience. When the disaster comes, they will understand; when they cannot mourn, they will recognize the sign. Veyda'atem ki ani Adonai YHWH: the recognition formula sealing the sign-act's revelatory purpose.
24.25: The fugitive's arrival: The text anticipates 33:21-22: when the fugitive arrives with news of Jerusalem's fall, Ezekiel's mouth will be opened (33:22). The sign-act in ch 24 is connected to the resolution in ch 33: the dumbness (3:26) that marked the judgment period will be lifted when the news arrives. Beyom qachti mehem et-mivsarim et-ma'oz tifaretam ('on the day I take from them their stronghold, their glorious joy'): the temple's loss is the trigger for the people's loss of the ability to mourn.
24.26: The survivor and the news: Beyom hahu yavo eleicha happalit lehashmi'a oznayim ('on that day a fugitive will come to you to make the ears hear'): the anticipated arrival of the news-bearer of 33:21. The function of the survivor is specifically to "make the ears hear" — to break the silence of non-mourning with the factual report. The suspension between the sign-act of ch 24 and its resolution in ch 33 creates a structural gap in the book that the reader lives through, mirroring the exiles' suspension between the sign and its fulfillment.
24.27: The opening of the mouth: Bayom hahu yippatach picha et-happalit ('on that day your mouth will be opened with the fugitive'): the sealing of Ezekiel's speech (3:26-27) is finally undone. The period of constrained prophetic speech — giving oracles only when the divine word came, unable to speak freely — ends with the confirmation of the fall. Velo teddom od ('and you will be silent no more'): the new era of free prophetic speech, which corresponds to the restoration oracles of chs 34-48, is inaugurated by the arrival of the news that makes the judgment complete.

25.1: Chapters 25-32 are the 'oracles against foreign nations' — a standard feature of classical prophecy (Isaiah 13-23; Jeremiah 46-51; Amos 1-2). Ezekiel's arrangement places the nations-oracles between the Jerusalem judgment (ch 1-24) and the restoration promises (ch 33-48). The nations are judged in a geographic encirclement of Israel: Ammon (east), Moab (southeast), Edom (south), Philistia (west), then the major powers Tyre, Sidon, and Egypt. The pattern suggests comprehensive judgment of all nations hostile to YHWH's purposes for Israel.
25.2: Ammon: East of the Jordan River and north of Moab, the Ammonites were perennial enemies of Israel from the Judges period (Judg 10-11) through the monarchy (1 Sam 11; 2 Sam 10-12; 1 Kgs 11:1-8). They occupied the region east of the Jordan that is modern Jordan, with their capital at Rabbah (modern Amman). Ammon's crime against Judah during the fall of Jerusalem is documented in multiple sources: Jeremiah 40:14 mentions the Ammonite king Baalis plotting against Gedaliah; Zephaniah 2:8-9 condemns Ammon's mockery of Israel.
25.3: "Aha!" over the sanctuary and the land: Ya'an emrech he'ach el-miqdashi ki nichlal ('because you said "Aha!" over my sanctuary when it was profaned'): the specific crime of Ammon is verbal — gleeful mockery of Israel's loss. The word he'ach is an exclamation of malicious pleasure at another's misfortune (cf. Ps 35:21: 'Aha! Aha! Our eyes have seen it!'). Ammon profited psychologically and perhaps politically from Jerusalem's fall: with Judah gone, the Transjordanian buffer against Babylonian westward expansion disappeared.
25.4: Judgment: becoming pasture for desert peoples: Ve'avi'a otach livnei qedem lemorashah ('I will give you to the people of the east as a possession'): the bnei qedem (sons of the east = desert nomads of the Syrian-Arabian desert) will take over Ammon's territory. Historical fulfillment: the Ammonite state gradually collapsed in the 5th-4th centuries BCE as Arab nomadic groups moved into the Transjordanian plateau. By the Hellenistic period, Ammonite national identity had largely dissolved. The avilim (pastoral encampments) and gederot tzon (sheep folds) replacing Ammon's cities.
25.5: Rabbah as a pasture and Ammon as a sheepfold: Venatati et-Rabbah lineveh-gemalim ('I will make Rabbah a pasture for camels'): the capital city (modern Amman) reduced to grazing land. The camel-pasture for Rabbah and the sheep-fold for Ammon's territory in general represents the reversal from settled urban civilization to nomadic pasture use. The yedatem ki-ani YHWH closes the oracle for Ammon — even the nations receive the recognition formula.
25.6: Hand-clapping and foot-stamping: Ki koh amar Adonai YHWH ya'an mehacha kapecha veraqa beraglecha ('because you clapped your hands and stamped your feet'): the physical expression of malicious glee at Israel's fall. These were culturally recognized gestures of contempt and celebration at an enemy's downfall. Ammon's physical expression of pleasure at the fall of Israel's land is the second specific accusation alongside the verbal "Aha!" of v.3. Both word and body celebrated Jerusalem's destruction.
25.7: Ammon "cut off from the peoples": Venatati otcha levaz lagoyim ('I will give you as plunder to nations'): the reversal of v.3 — Ammon will be the object of exactly the treatment they wished for Israel. Vehikhratticha min-ha'ammim ('and I will cut you off from the peoples'): national extinction. The thoroughness of Ammon's punishment corresponds to the completeness of their rejoicing at Israel's humiliation. The yedatem formula: even in judgment Ammon will be compelled to recognize YHWH.
25.8: Moab: South of Ammon and east of the Dead Sea, Moab occupied the plateau region of modern central Jordan. The Moabites were traditional enemies (Num 22-25; Judg 3:12-30) with a complex relationship to Israel — the Ruth story shows the possibility of incorporation, but the general prophetic tradition sees Moab as hostile. Moab's crime: amar Beit Yehudah kekhol-hagoyim ('saying: the house of Judah is like all the other nations'). This is a theological claim denying Israel's covenant distinctiveness — asserting that YHWH's people are no different from any other nation.
25.9: Moab's cities as the border: The opening of Moab's flank (pitachti et-ketef Mo'av me'arim) refers to the exposure of Moab's border cities — Beth-jeshimoth, Baal-meon, and Kiriathaim. These were among Moab's most important border cities, visible from the eastern shore of the Dead Sea. The symbolic significance: Moab's frontier, the thing that defined its territorial identity, is exposed to invasion. The pride of Moab — its identity as a distinct nation — is attacked at its most fundamental level.
25.10: Moab given to desert peoples alongside Ammon: Livnei qedem al-bnei Ammon netatiha lemorashah ('to the people of the east against the Ammonites I will give it as a possession'): Ammon and Moab share the same judgment — both Transjordanian kingdoms being given over to the nomadic peoples from the Syrian-Arabian desert. The shared fate of the two eastern neighbors reflects their shared sin of contempt for Israel's covenant identity.
25.11: Judgments upon Moab: Venasiti bifalachim beMo'av ('I will execute judgments on Moab'): the same judgment formula as for Ammon. The recognition formula (veyad'u ki-ani YHWH) closes both the Moab section and — since the nations form a parallel structure — the entire Ammon-Moab unit. The two Transjordanian kingdoms are judged together as representatives of the nations east of Israel who denied Israel's covenant distinctiveness.
25.12: Edom: The Edomites, descendants of Esau, occupied the mountainous region south of the Dead Sea (Seir/Petra area). Their hatred of Israel went back to the Jacob-Esau conflict (Gen 25-27; 36) and intensified during the monarchy period (2 Sam 8:13-14; 1 Kgs 11:14-22). But Edom's most notorious act was their collaboration with or at minimum failure to help during the Babylonian destruction of Jerusalem. Psalm 137:7 records: 'Remember, YHWH, what the Edomites did on the day Jerusalem fell: "Tear it down," they cried, "tear it down to its foundations!"'
25.13: Judgment on Edom from Teman to Dedan: Miteman usedda uvederekh vetocham yippolu vecherev ('from Teman to Dedan they shall fall by the sword'): Teman was in the north of Edomite territory (cf. Amos 1:12; Obad 9); Dedan was in the south (near modern NW Saudi Arabia). The geographic extremes designate the full territory of Edom. Avi'a et-cherev be'Edom ('I will lay my sword against Edom'): the divine instrument of judgment named specifically for Edom — not desert nomads (as for Ammon/Moab) but military conquest.
25.14: Judgment through Israel: Venatatiy et-niqmati beEdom beyad ami Yisrael ('and I will lay my vengeance on Edom by the hand of my people Israel'): uniquely, Edom's judgment will come through Israel — not through the Babylonians or desert peoples. This anticipates the future restoration of Israel as an active agent in the world, capable of executing divine judgment on those who victimized them. Ve'asu veEdom ke'api uche'amati: they will know YHWH's anger and wrath. Historical fulfillment in the Maccabean period: the Idumeans (Edomites) were forcibly incorporated into the Jewish state by John Hyrcanus (c.125 BCE).
25.15: Philistia: The coastal strip of southern Canaan — the Philistine pentapolis of Gaza, Ashkelon, Ashdod, Ekron, and Gath — had been Israel's primary military rival from the Judges period through the Davidic conquest. The Philistines' crime: la'asot neqamah beshinat nefesh leshachet eivat olam ('acting in revenge with perpetual enmity to destroy with longstanding hatred'). Eivat olam (perpetual/ancient enmity) is the same phrase used for Edom in 35:5 — the ancient hostility dating from the period of Israelite settlement or earlier.
25.16: Cut off the Cherethites, destroy the seacoast: Vehikhrati et-Keretim ('I will cut off the Cherethites'): the Cherethites (Kreti) are a Philistine subgroup (cf. 1 Sam 30:14; 2 Sam 8:18 where David's royal bodyguard included Kerethites and Pelethites — possibly Philistine mercenaries). The seacoast remainder (yeter chof hayam) — the entire Mediterranean coastal strip controlled by the Philistines. The judgment on Philistia completes the geographic encirclement: with Ammon (east), Moab (southeast), Edom (south), and Philistia (west) all under judgment, Israel is surrounded by the nations receiving divine punishment.
25.17: Great vengeance with furious rebukes: Venasiti bahem niqamot gedolot betochechot chemah ('and I will execute great vengeance upon them with furious rebukes'): the strongest judgment language in the nations-oracle sequence. Veyad'u ki-ani YHWH: the recognition formula closes all four oracles and the entire chapter. The structural conclusion of the chapter-25 oracles: every surrounding nation — the four immediate neighbors of Israel — will be brought to the recognition of YHWH through the experience of divine judgment. The universal scope of the recognition formula means that YHWH's self-disclosure is not limited to the covenant people but encompasses all the nations in Israel's immediate orbit.

29.1: The date header — “tenth year, tenth month, twelfth day” — corresponds to January 7, 587 BCE (Julian), approximately 18 months into Nebuchadnezzar's siege of Jerusalem (which began in December 589 BCE). This is the fifth of the six dated Egypt oracles and one of only three oracles in Ezekiel dated within the siege-period of 589-587 BCE (alongside 24:1 and 30:20). The context is critical: Jerusalem is surrounded, the Egyptian army under Hophra/Apries has moved toward Judah to relieve it (Jer 37:5), and the oracle against Egypt arrives at precisely the moment when exiles might pin their hopes on Egyptian intervention.
29.2: “Set your face against Pharaoh” — the same confrontation formula used against Jerusalem (Ezek 6:2), Judah (21:2), Ammon (25:2), Tyre (28:21), and Sidon (28:22). By applying the identical formula to Egypt that YHWH used against his own people and their neighbors, Ezekiel places Egypt within the same theological framework: no nation stands outside YHWH's jurisdiction. The formula originated in Israelite siege rhetoric (the commander “set his face against” a fortified city) and became Ezekiel's characteristic expression of prophetic-divine opposition. “Prophesy against him and against all Egypt” extends the judgment from the individual ruler to the entire nation — Pharaoh's pride implicates Egypt.
29.3: Pharaoh king of Egypt is identified as “the great sea creature lying in the midst of his rivers” (hatannin hagadol harovets betokh yeoreiv). Tannin (sea creature/monster) is the chaos-dragon of ANE cosmology — cognate with the Ugaritic tnn who fights Baal, and related to the Babylonian Tiamat whom Marduk slays. In Egyptian iconography, Pharaoh was associated with the Nile crocodile (Sobek) and with the serpent-dragon Apophis, the enemy of Re. Ezekiel collapses these traditions: Pharaoh is a chaos creature whose domain is the Nile. The boast “My Nile is my own; I made it for myself” (li yeori vaani asitini) echoes the Pharaonic theology that the annual Nile flood was mediated by the king's divine status — the pharaoh's ritual power was believed to sustain the Nile's fertility. This is the precise claim YHWH will refute: the Nile belongs to YHWH, not Pharaoh, and YHWH will prove it by removing Pharaoh from it.
29.4: Fish-hook imagery (“I will put hooks in your jaws”) depicts YHWH as the divine fisher-king who hauls out the chaos monster from its native element. This inverts the normal ANE imagery where the great fish is a symbol of abundance and divine provision — here it becomes the means of humiliation. The fish clinging to Pharaoh's scales are his people and the armies dependent on him — they will be dragged out together. The same hook imagery appears in Amos 4:2 (Israel will be “taken away with hooks”) and in Ezek 38:4 (YHWH will put hooks in Gog's jaws). In Egyptian military iconography, prisoners were often depicted with hooks through their lips or noses — a deliberately humiliating reversal of Egyptian conquering-imagery.
29.5: Thrown into the wilderness — the reversal of the Exodus. In the Exodus, Israel was freed from Egypt and led through the wilderness to the land; here, Egypt's king is expelled from his river to the wilderness and left unburied. “Not be gathered or collected” (lo-teasef velo tikavets) — proper burial was a deep concern in the ancient world; unburied corpses were the ultimate disgrace (Deut 28:26; Jer 7:33; 8:2). Leaving Pharaoh's body as “food to the beasts of the field and birds of the air” is the specific covenant-curse of Deut 28:26 applied to Egypt's king. The wilderness-exposure imagery also evokes the fate of Egypt's army in the Red Sea — another reversal: previously Egypt's enemies drowned in the sea; now Egypt is exposed in the desert.
29.6: “All the inhabitants of Egypt will know that I am the LORD, because they have been a staff of reed to the house of Israel” — the “knowledge formula” (veyadeu ki ani YHWH) appears 72 times in Ezekiel and marks every major judgment and restoration oracle. YHWH's actions produce knowledge of who he is — his identity is revealed through historical events. The “staff of reed” (mikkel qaneh) metaphor already appeared in the Assyrian threat to Hezekiah (2 Kings 18:21 = Isa 36:6): “you are relying on Egypt, that broken reed of a staff, which will pierce the hand of any man who leans on it.” Ezekiel applies the same image to Judah's continued reliance on Egypt despite that earlier warning — 130 years later, the same mistake, the same result.
29.7: The reed staff imagery is extended mechanically: when Israel “grasped you with their hand, you splintered and tore their shoulder.” Egypt's military intervention on Judah's behalf (Hophra's march toward Jerusalem in 588 BCE, documented in Jer 37:5-11) briefly caused Nebuchadnezzar to lift the siege — but Egypt withdrew without engaging, and the siege resumed. The physical image (a walking-staff that splinters cuts the user's palm; a weight-bearing staff that breaks wrenches the shoulder) perfectly captures Egypt's half-hearted intervention: Egypt's support caused damage rather than help, because Hophra withdrew at the critical moment, forcing Judah to re-face the Babylonian siege alone and with diminished hope.
29.8: The “sword against you” formula (veheveiti aleykha cherev) is the divine-warrior declaration: YHWH himself is the attacker, using Nebuchadnezzar's sword as his instrument (made explicit in 29:19). The cutting off of “both man and beast” (adam uvehemah) mirrors the covenant-curse language of Lev 26:22 (beasts cutting off livestock) and Deut 28:31 (loss of animals). The destruction extends beyond human casualties to the agricultural economy — Egypt's cattle herds grazed on the Nile's floodplain, and their destruction signals the collapse of Egypt's economic foundations.
29.9: The knowledge formula (“they will know that I am the LORD”) appears again, this time grounding the judgment in Pharaoh's specific sin: the claim of Nile-ownership and self-creation. The theological point is sharp — Egypt's judgment is not random but proportionate: Egypt claimed divine powers over the Nile, and YHWH will demonstrate that the Nile is his to give or take. The same logic governs all the Egypt oracles: Egypt's fall is not merely political conquest but a demonstration of YHWH's exclusive sovereignty over creation, including the natural resources that Egypt falsely attributed to its own divine king.
29.10: “From Migdol to Syene, as far as the border of Cush” — Migdol (Migdol) was a fortress city on Egypt's northeastern border in the Sinai delta region (identified with Tell el-Borg or Tell el-Abqa); it was also the location where some Jewish refugees from Jerusalem settled after 586 BCE (Jer 44:1). Syene (Aswan, at the first cataract of the Nile) was Egypt's southern border with Nubia/Cush, site of the famous Elephantine Jewish colony. The phrase is therefore equivalent to “from north to south” — the entire length of the Nile valley. The rhetorical gesture (naming the extreme boundaries) is the same as Israel's “from Dan to Beersheba” — totality expressed through polar extremes.
29.11: “No human foot will pass through it, no animal's foot will pass through it, and it will not be inhabited for forty years” — the forty-year desolation is the covenant-discipline number (forty years of wilderness wandering; forty days on Sinai; forty days of Elijah's journey). The desolation is total: no foot — animal or human — will traverse it. This hyperbolic language follows the pattern of the Sodom-and-Gomorrah oracle in Isa 13:20 (Babylon: “it will never be inhabited or lived in for all generations; no Arab will pitch his tent there”). Egypt was indeed weakened after Nebuchadnezzar's campaign (568-567 BCE) but was not literally uninhabited for forty years — the prophetic language uses covenant-number (forty) to signal the totality and divine purposiveness of the discipline.
29.12: The desolation of Egypt “in the midst of desolated lands” places Egypt within the broader landscape of Babylonian conquest — Judah, Phoenicia, and the surrounding nations were all in various states of devastation. Egypt's cities “among ruined cities” (bevein arim chorevot) reflects the actual condition of the eastern Mediterranean world after Nebuchadnezzar's campaigns: Tyre besieged for 13 years, Jerusalem destroyed, Ammon and Moab subjugated. The scattering of Egyptians among the nations mirrors exactly what happened to Israel — YHWH treats Egypt with the same covenant-curse logic he applied to his own people, universalizing the judgment structure.
29.13: “Yet” (ki koh amar Adonai YHWH veqibatzti) — the restoration promise interrupts the desolation oracle in the same pattern as the restoration promises within the Israel-judgment oracles (Ezek 11:17; 20:41-42; 36:24). Egypt will be gathered from the nations at the end of forty years — a reversal of its scattering. The “gathering from the peoples” (qibatzti) language is the same used for Israel's eschatological restoration (20:34, 41; 28:25; 34:13; 36:24; 37:21; 38:8). YHWH's gathering formula is not exclusive to Israel — it belongs to his sovereign management of all nations, and Egypt receives a form of it, though on much reduced terms.
29.14: Restoration to “Pathros, the land of their origin” — Pathros (Hebrew Patros, Egyptian Pa-to-Res, “the southern land”) is Upper Egypt, the ancient heartland of Egyptian civilization centered on Thebes. The 26th Dynasty (the Saite period, ca. 664-525 BCE) had its origins in Lower Egypt (the Delta), ruling a reunified Egypt from Sais. Pathros as origin-land suggests Ezekiel envisions Egypt returning to its ancient core territory rather than dominating the Delta and the near eastern corridor. The “lowly kingdom” (mamlekhet shefalah) is not an insult but a categorical diminution: Egypt will persist as a nation but will never again be the regional hegemon that Israel's kings sought as an ally.
29.15: “The humblest of kingdoms, and it will no longer exalt itself over the nations” — this is fulfilled in broad historical outline. After the Babylonian and then Persian conquest (Cambyses invaded Egypt in 525 BCE), Egypt never again achieved independent regional hegemony. It remained a wealthy but politically subordinate province: Persian satrapy (525-332 BCE), Macedonian/Ptolemaic kingdom (332-30 BCE), and Roman province (30 BCE). The “I will diminish them” (umitatim) formula is the precise reversal of the reed-staff offense: Egypt claimed to prop up others; YHWH will make it so small it cannot prop up anyone, including itself.
