The oath formula guarantees that the following verdict (v. 10) will be executed without qualification.
34.9: The second summons of the shepherds to hear the word of YHWH repeats v. 7 verbatim, creating the Hebrew rhetorical pattern of double announcement before a significant declaration. The repetition is not redundant but emphatic — the formal juridical structure of a legal proceeding requires that the accused be clearly addressed before the verdict is read. The shepherds are explicitly identified as the defendants in YHWH’s court.
34.10: “Behold I am against the shepherds (hineni el-haro-im)” — the adversative formula hineni el (I am against) is Ezekiel’s characteristic announcement of divine opposition to a specific entity (13:8; 21:8; 26:3; 28:22). “I will hold them accountable for my flock (uviqashti et-tzoni mi-yadam)” — the verb baqash (to seek, to require) used earlier for the shepherds’ failure to seek the scattered flock is now turned against the shepherds: YHWH will require (baqash) the flock from their hand. The juridical reversal is precise. “I will rescue my flock from their mouths that it may not be food for them” — the same “food” vocabulary (v. 5) is now applied to the shepherds themselves eating the sheep, not the wild beasts.
34.11: “For thus says the Lord GOD: Behold, I myself will search for my sheep and seek them out (vedarashti et-tzoni uveqartim).” The emphatic “I myself (ani)” marks the climactic shift from the failed human shepherds to the divine shepherd who personally takes up the task. The two verbs (darash, to search diligently; baqar, to seek/examine) are the mirror of the human shepherds’ failure in v. 6 (“no one to search or seek”). YHWH’s personal assumption of the shepherding role is the radical claim of the passage: the divine king of Israel will do what Israel’s human kings failed to do. John 10:11 (“I am the good shepherd”) is the NT’s claim that Jesus is the enactment of this YHWH-as-shepherd promise in human form.
34.12: “As a shepherd seeks out his flock when some of his sheep have been scattered, so will I seek out my sheep.” The simile draws on pastoral practice: the owner of a scattered flock personally combs the terrain to locate each missing animal. “I will rescue them from all the places where they have been scattered on a day of clouds and thick darkness (beyom anan va-arafel).” The phrase “day of clouds and thick darkness” is the vocabulary of theophany (Exod 19:9; Deut 4:11) and divine judgment (Joel 2:2; Zeph 1:15) — the gathering will happen precisely in the moment of historical catastrophe, not despite it but through it.
34.13: “I will bring them out from the peoples and gather them from the countries (vegibbatstim min-ha-aratzot)” — the new exodus/restoration language that governs Ezekiel 33-48. The gathering of the dispersed (qibbuts galuyot, ingathering of exiles) is the prophetic program announced here and developed in chs. 36-37. “I will bring them into their own land. I will feed them on the mountains of Israel, by the streams, and in all the inhabited places.” The mountains of Israel — which in ch. 35 will be contrasted with Mount Seir’s desolation — become the site of restoration pastoral abundance.
34.14: “I will feed them in good pasture, and their fold shall be on the mountain heights of Israel (bemaromi Yisrael).” The mountain heights of Israel are the geographic inverse of the “every high hill” on which the sheep wandered leaderless in v. 6. The restoration involves returning to the same terrain, but under YHWH’s direct shepherding rather than human negligence. “On rich pasture (mireh shamen) they shall feed on the mountains of Israel.” The restored covenant community will enjoy the actual material blessing of the promised land — the covenant promise of Deut 8:7-9 fulfilled after the judgment period.
34.15: “I myself will be the shepherd of my flock (ani er-eh tzoni), and I myself will make them lie down (ani arbi-itsem), declares the Lord GOD.” The doubled ani (I myself) is emphatic repetition for rhetorical force: YHWH is not delegating the shepherding role but personally performing it. The phrase “make them lie down” (arbi-itsem) evokes Psalm 23:2 (“He makes me lie down in green pastures”) — Psalm 23 is the lyrical meditation on exactly this promise, where YHWH-as-shepherd performs each action that Israel’s failed shepherds neglected. The oracle of ch. 34 is the juridical-prophetic prose behind the Psalm’s poetic confessions.
34.16: YHWH’s shepherd duties as the positive fulfillment of the negative catalog of the human shepherds’ failures (v. 4): “I will seek the lost, bring back the strayed, bind up the injured, and strengthen the weak.” Each item directly reverses the failure-catalog. “But the fat and the strong I will destroy (ve-hashemen ve-hachazaq eshamed); I will feed them in justice (mishpat).” The intra-flock judgment — between those who exploited the weak and the vulnerable who were exploited — introduces the second movement of the chapter (vv. 17-22). YHWH’s shepherding is not mere benevolence but includes active justice on behalf of the marginalized.
34.17: “As for you, my flock, thus says the Lord GOD: Behold, I judge between sheep and sheep, between rams and male goats.” The oracle extends beyond the external human shepherds to the internal dynamics of the community itself. The “rams and male goats” (eilim u-attudim) — the dominant animals within the flock — represent the powerful members of the covenant community who took advantage of their position. In pastoral practice, dominant rams push less dominant sheep away from water and pasture. The social-class application is clear: the wealthy and powerful within Israel exploited the weak and poor.
34.18: “Is it not enough for you to feed on the good pasture, that you must tread down with your feet the rest of your pasture?” The metaphor of fouling the water supply and trampling the pasture captures the social reality of elite economic behavior: not merely consuming the good, but actively destroying what others could have used. Muddy water and trampled pasture are useless to those who come after the dominant animals. The economic application: the wealthy not only monopolized resources but destroyed the productive capacity of the commons through their practices.
34.19: “Must my flock eat what you have trodden with your feet, and drink what you have muddied with your feet?” The rhetorical question completes the social-class analysis: the poor receive the remnants degraded by the rich — trampled food and fouled water. This imagery is the pastoral-allegorical expression of the prophetic social critique that runs from Amos through Isaiah through Jeremiah: the economic arrangements of pre-exile Israel systematically disadvantaged the poor while benefiting the rich. Ezekiel places the responsibility for this directly on the dominant members of the community.
34.20: “Therefore, thus says the Lord GOD to them: Behold, I myself will judge between the fat sheep and the lean sheep.” The lachen (therefore) introduces YHWH’s personal judicial intervention in the intra-community social conflict. “The fat sheep and the lean sheep” (bein seh briah lish-seh razah) — fat (well-fed, economically privileged) versus lean (malnourished, economically marginalized). The divine judge takes the side of the lean sheep, not because they are morally superior, but because YHWH’s covenant justice includes active vindication of the marginalized against those who exploited them.
34.21: “Because you push with side and shoulder, and thrust at all the weak with your horns (qarneikem tenachu)” — the physical behavior of dominant rams in a flock, used to describe the social aggression of the powerful members of the community. The horn (qeren) is the symbol of power throughout the OT (Ps 75:4-5; Dan 7:8; Amos 6:13). “Until you have scattered them abroad (pitzztam el-chutz)” — the rich have contributed to the scattering of the poor through economic predation, mirroring (and perhaps causing) the national scattering that YHWH judged.
34.22: “I will rescue my flock, and they shall no longer be a prey (levo-az liviz).” The promise of protection extends the shepherd imagery to its covenantal fullness: the restored community will not be subject to either external predators (other nations) or internal exploiters (the powerful within the community). “I will judge between sheep and sheep” — the repeated formula (first in v. 17, now in v. 22) closes the intra-community judgment section and introduces the Messianic promise of vv. 23-24. The establishment of justice within the community is the precondition for the appointment of the shepherd-king.
34.23: “And I will set up over them one shepherd, my servant David (eved david), and he shall feed them.” After the long account of YHWH’s personal shepherding, this verse introduces the human agent through whom that shepherding will be exercised: “my servant David.” David had been dead for four centuries by Ezekiel’s time; “my servant David” is therefore the Messianic figure who inherits David’s royal office and covenant promises (2 Sam 7:12-16: “I will raise up your offspring after you”). The Davidic covenant is the framework: the one shepherd is not David personally but the Davidic heir who fulfils what David’s dynasty was meant to be. Isaiah 11:1 (“a shoot from the stump of Jesse”), Jer 23:5 (“a righteous Branch for David”), and Zech 3:8; 6:12 (“my servant the Branch”) all operate within this same Davidic-Messianic promise framework.
34.24: “And I, the LORD, will be their God, and my servant David shall be nasi (prince, leader) among them.” The term nasi (prince, leader, elevated one) is Ezekiel’s preferred title for the Messianic ruler rather than melech (king) — Ezekiel uses nasi for the restored Davidic leader throughout the temple vision (44:3; 45:7-17; 46:2-18; 48:21-22). The distinction may reflect a deliberate distancing from the failed melech-ship of Judah’s kings: the restored leader will be a nasi under YHWH’s kingship rather than an autonomous melech. The covenant formula “I will be their God” (ani YHWH eloheichem) closes the verse with the relational core of all the promises.
34.25: “I will make a covenant of peace (berit shalom) with them.” The covenant of peace appears in Ezekiel at 34:25 and 37:26 and in Isaiah at 54:10 and 66:12 — it is the eschatological covenant that fulfills and exceeds the Mosaic covenant. The berit shalom (covenant of peace/completeness/well-being) uses shalom in its full sense of total well-being, not merely the absence of conflict. “Banish wild beasts from the land (vehishhbati chayah ra-ah min-ha-aretz)” — wild animals as a covenant-curse symbol (Lev 26:22: “I will send wild beasts against you”; Ezek 14:15) are now removed as a covenant-blessing sign. The wilderness becomes safe, the forest navigable.
34.26: “I will make them and the places around my hill a blessing (berachah), and I will send down the showers in their season (geshem bito).” The seasonal rains are the most fundamental blessing in Palestine’s agricultural calendar: the early rains (October-November) for planting and the latter rains (March-April) for grain. Their regularity was the covenant blessing of Deut 11:14 (“I will give the rain of your land in its season”) and their withholding the curse (Deut 28:24; 1 Kings 17’s drought under Ahab). “Showers of blessing (gishme berachah yihyu)” — the phrase is deliberately comprehensive: the rain itself is theologically characterized as blessing, not merely as meteorological fact.
34.27: “The trees of the field shall yield their fruit, and the earth shall yield its increase.” The restored land’s agricultural productivity is the material expression of covenant restoration (Lev 26:4: “the land shall yield its increase and the trees of the field shall yield their fruit”). “They shall be secure in their land. And they shall know that I am the LORD, when I break the bars of their yoke (beshivri et-motot ullam).” The “bars of the yoke” is the standard ANE metaphor for political subjugation under a foreign power (Jer 27:2-12: the yoke of Babylon). Breaking the yoke = release from political servitude = recognition of YHWH as the one who acts in history to liberate his people.
34.28: “They shall no longer be a prey to the nations, nor shall the beasts of the land devour them.” The double security from both political (nations) and natural (wild beasts) threats completes the reversal of the covenant curses (Lev 26:22, 25). “They shall dwell securely (lavetech) and none shall make them afraid (ein machared).” The phrase lavetech... ein machared is the formula of covenant security that appears in Lev 26:6 and throughout the prophetic restoration oracles (Jer 23:6; 33:16; Mic 4:4; Zeph 3:13). Security from fear is the fundamental experiential quality of restored covenant life.
34.29: “And I will provide for them a renowned planting (matta leshemah), so that they shall no more be consumed with famine in the land.” Matta leshemah (a planting for/of reputation, a renowned plantation) — the phrase is unique to Ezekiel and difficult. It likely refers to an established, productive agricultural community whose abundance is recognized by surrounding nations (“renowned” in the sense of known for its fruitfulness). “No longer suffer the disgrace of the nations (lo yis-u od cherpat hagoyim)” — the shame of exile and dispossession has been the national disgrace; the restoration removes it. Honor-shame was not merely an emotional category in ancient Israel but a social reality with economic and political dimensions.
34.30: “They shall know that I, the LORD their God, am with them (otam), and that they, the house of Israel, are my people (ami), declares the Lord GOD.” The mutual covenant formula (“I am their God / they are my people”) is the relational core of all the preceding material restoration (land, rain, security, food). It is the Sinai covenant formula (Exod 6:7; Lev 26:12) given its eschatological fullness: the mechanical national covenant becomes a known relational reality. The NT fulfillment is the Incarnation and Pentecost: Emmanuel (God-with-us) and the Spirit’s indwelling are the “I am with them” realized in person and power.
34.31: “You are my sheep, human sheep of my pasture (tzon mar-iti tzon adam atten), and I am your God, declares the Lord GOD.” The closing verse’s phrase tzon adam (human sheep, literally “sheep of humans / humankind”) may be distinguishing Israel from literal sheep while maintaining the metaphor, or emphasizing the personal relational ownership: human beings who are YHWH’s flock, not merely livestock. The closing covenant declaration “I am your God” in the mouth of YHWH directly echoes “I am the LORD your God” from the Decalogue (Exod 20:2) — the shepherd oracle ends where the covenant began: with YHWH’s self-identifying declaration of covenant ownership.

35.1: The oracular formula introduces ch. 35, an oracle against Mount Seir (= Edom). Its placement between the shepherd oracle of ch. 34 and the restoration oracle for Israel’s mountains in ch. 36 is structurally significant: Edom’s desolation and Israel’s restoration are the two sides of the same divine act. Ch. 35’s desolation of Mount Seir (vv. 3, 4, 7, 9) provides the dark foil for ch. 36’s promise to Israel’s mountains.
35.2: “Son of man, set your face toward Mount Seir and prophesy against it (hinnavei alav).” Mount Seir (the mountainous region of Edom, southeast of the Dead Sea, modern Jordan) is identified in Genesis with Esau and his descendants (Gen 36:8: “Esau settled in the hill country of Seir; Esau is Edom”). The Jacob-Esau conflict that began in the womb (Gen 25:22-23) and continued through the sale of the birthright (Gen 25:29-34) and the stolen blessing (Gen 27) is the ancestral backstory for the prophetic enmity between Israel and Edom. The directional command “set your face toward” is Ezekiel’s standard prophetic-confrontation posture (6:2; 13:17; 25:2).
35.3: “Behold, I am against you, Mount Seir (hineni eleicha har seir).” The adversative formula hineni el (I am against you) that Ezekiel uses for the most severe divine declarations of hostility (against the false prophets, 13:8; against the nations, 26:3; 28:22; 29:3). The promise to “stretch out my hand against you (venatateti yadei aleicha)” uses the standard ANE gesture of divine power-in-action (the “outstretched arm” of divine agency, cf. Exod 3:20; 6:6; Deut 4:34). “A desolation and a waste (shemmah umeshammah)” — the identical double formula used for the land of Israel in 33:28, now directed at Edom.
35.4: “Your cities I will lay waste (arev arev), and you shall become a desolation.” The judgment of Edom’s cities fulfills the earlier oracle against Edom in Ezek 25:12-14 and the entire book of Obadiah. The recognition formula “you shall know that I am the LORD” is used here for an enemy nation: YHWH’s identity is disclosed to Edom through judgment, not salvation. The same recognition formula that in chs. 33-37 prompts Israel’s restoration produces a different recognition in Edom: acknowledgment of YHWH’s power over their destruction.
35.5: “Because you cherished perpetual enmity (eivat olam) and gave over the people of Israel to the power of the sword (higarta livnei Yisrael al-yadei charev).” Eivat olam (perpetual enmity, literally “enmity of eternity/long-standing enmity”) is the causal charge. The Edom-Israel hostility had deep historical roots in the Exodus period: Edom refused Israel passage through their territory (Num 20:14-21) and Saul’s wars with Edom (1 Sam 14:47). “At the time of their calamity, at the time of their final punishment (beet eidam beet avon kets)” — the specific accusation: Edom exploited Israel’s moment of greatest weakness (the 586 BCE Babylonian conquest) to join in the slaughter. Obadiah 10-14 is the most detailed account of Edom’s specific crimes during Jerusalem’s fall: standing aloof, rejoicing, looting, cutting off fugitives.
35.6: “As I live, declares the Lord GOD, I will destine you for blood (ledamim essa-cha), and blood shall pursue you (vedamim yirdephucha).” The talion principle in covenantal form: Edom handed Israel over to the sword; YHWH will make Edom the target of blood-guilt. “Since you did not hate bloodshed (vedamim lo sane-ta), bloodshed shall pursue you.” The moral logic of covenantal retribution: what a nation welcomes as a tool against others, they receive in return. The phrase “bloodshed shall pursue you” uses the image of blood-guilt as an active pursuer — blood-guilt was in ancient Israelite legal thought a kind of supernatural force that demanded satisfaction (Gen 4:10: “your brother’s blood cries to me from the ground”).
35.7: “I will make Mount Seir a waste and a desolation, and I will cut off from it all who come and go (veechrat mimenu over vashaV).” The phrase “cut off all who come and go” describes the complete end of normal human habitation and travel — the paths and routes through Edom’s territory will be abandoned. Edom’s strategic position astride the King’s Highway (the major north-south trade route along the Transjordanian plateau) made it a commercially important territory; cutting off traffic through it removes both the people and their economic function.
35.8: “I will fill its mountains with the slain (uchaleti et-hareicha chalalim).” The use of harim (mountains) for Edom’s terrain specifically anticipates the contrast with the “mountains of Israel” in ch. 36 — Edom’s mountains will be filled with the slain while Israel’s mountains will be filled with the returned people. “On your hills and in your valleys and in all your ravines those slain by the sword shall fall” — the comprehensive geographic formula (“hills, valleys, ravines”) emphasizes that no terrain feature of Edom escapes. The same geographic comprehensiveness applied to Israel’s restoration in 36:4 (“to the mountains and hills, the ravines and valleys”).
35.9: “I will make you a perpetual desolation (shemamot olam), and your cities shall not return to be inhabited.” Shemamot olam (perpetual/eternal desolation) is the strongest possible desolation language — it contrasts with Edom’s hope of possessing Israel’s land permanently (v. 10). The historical record: the Nabataean Arab kingdom that occupied Edom’s territory from the 4th century BCE effectively displaced the Edomite population. By the NT period, Idumea (the Hellenistic name for Edom) had been largely absorbed into Judea under John Hyrcanus (125 BCE), and the Edomite population as a distinct entity disappeared. The desolation oracle found its historical fulfillment in stages. The recognition formula closes the verse.
35.10: “Because you said, ‘These two nations and these two countries shall be mine, and we will possess them’ — although the LORD was there (va-YHWH sham hayah).” The two nations and two countries are Israel and Judah (or the northern and southern kingdoms). Edom’s territorial ambition at the moment of Israel’s collapse (586 BCE) is the second charge, supplementing the blood-guilt of v. 5. “Although the LORD was there” is the pregnant parenthetical that undermines Edom’s possession claim: Edom apparently concluded that YHWH had abandoned the land when Israel was defeated. But Ezekiel consistently insists that YHWH’s departure from the temple (chs. 10-11) was a temporary covenant judgment, not a permanent abandonment. YHWH’s presence in the land makes the land untransferable to another people.
35.11: “As I live, declares the Lord GOD, I will deal with you according to the anger and envy that you showed through your hatred of them.” The lex talionis applied at the emotional-motivational level: Edom’s anger and envy against Israel become the measure of YHWH’s response against Edom. “I will make myself known among them when I judge you (venodati vahem beshofteki otach)” — YHWH’s self-revelation through the judgment of Edom is for the benefit of Israel: Israel will see and know YHWH through his vindication of them against their ancient enemy. Divine judgment of enemies is, in the covenantal framework, simultaneously defense of the victim.
35.12: “And you shall know that I am the LORD. I have heard all the insults (neatzu-teicha) you hurled against the mountains of Israel, saying, ‘They are laid desolate; they are given to us to devour.’” The “mountains of Israel” that Edom gloated over in desolation are the very mountains that will be addressed in YHWH’s restoration oracle of ch. 36. Edom’s speech against the mountains of Israel (“they are ours to devour”) is the direct foil for YHWH’s speech to the mountains of Israel (“I will restore you”, 36:1-15). The insults (neatzu) have been heard — prophetic oracles against Edom appear in Isa 34; Jer 49:7-22; Amos 1:11-12; Obadiah; Mal 1:2-5.
35.13: “And you spoke arrogantly against me with your mouth and multiplied your words against me; I heard it.” Edom’s insults against Israel’s mountains (v. 12) are simultaneously insults against YHWH: since YHWH is identified with the land and people of Israel, contempt for Israel’s desolation is contempt for YHWH’s apparent defeat. The phrase “multiplied your words against me (higgadetm alay pi-chem)” escalates from insults to arrogant boasting against the divine sovereign. “I heard it (ani shamati)” — the divine hearing of defiant speech is consistently the trigger for prophetic judgment in the OT (Ps 94:7-11; Isa 37:28-29; Ezek 25:3, 8, 12).
35.14: “Thus says the Lord GOD: While the whole earth rejoices (besimchath kol-ha-aretz), I will make you a desolation.” The cosmic reversal: at the moment of Edom’s desolation, the entire earth celebrates. The phrase “the whole earth rejoices” anticipates the restoration of Israel and the nations’ response to it (cf. Ps 96:11-12; Isa 44:23; 49:13). Edom’s desolation is not a private event but a cosmic moment in which YHWH’s justice is publicly displayed. The contrast with Edom’s rejoicing over Israel’s desolation (v. 15) completes the reversal: what Edom did to Israel, YHWH does to Edom, while all the earth watches.
35.15: “As you rejoiced over the inheritance of the house of Israel because it was desolate, so I will deal with you. You shall be desolate, Mount Seir, and all Edom, all of it.” The precise lex talionis closure: the proportionality of Edom’s desolation to their rejoicing over Israel’s desolation. “Then they shall know that I am the LORD.” The recognition formula closes the oracle as it has closed the other judgment oracles throughout Ezekiel. In the structure of chs. 35-36, Edom learns YHWH’s identity through their judgment (35:15), while Israel learns YHWH’s identity through their restoration (36:11) — two modes of the same divine self-disclosure that constitutes the theological purpose of the entire historical movement.

36.1: Chapter 36 opens with an oracle "to the mountains of Israel" — the direct counterpart to the oracle against the mountains in ch 6. Where ch 6 announced desolation for the mountains that hosted the idol-worship, ch 36 announces restoration for those same mountains now shamed by enemy occupation. The structural pairing of chs 6 and 36 is one of the major literary symmetries of the book: the same geographical entity that received the judgment oracle now receives the restoration oracle.
36.2: The enemy's taunt — "Aha! The ancient heights have become our possession" — is the specific insult addressed in the restoration oracle. The "ancient heights" (bamot ʿolam) refers to the highlands of Israel, the geographic heart of the land. The nations who said "Aha!" over Israel's desolation are the same nations condemned in ch 25 (Ammon, 25:3). The restoration is in part a vindication against these taunters.
36.3: Being made desolate and pursued from every side so that the nations talked about Israel — the diaspora experience of national humiliation is the context for the restoration promise. The Babylonian exile produced a community perceived by surrounding nations as abandoned by its God. The nations' theological conclusion (that YHWH had failed or been defeated) is precisely the "profaning of YHWH's holy name" that drives the restoration in vv22-23.
36.4: The address to the mountains, hills, ravines, valleys, desolate wastes, and forsaken cities — the comprehensive geographic inventory of the land as the addressee mirrors the comprehensive destruction oracle of ch 6. The mountains of Israel are promised that they will bear fruit for YHWH's people Israel; the land itself is a participant in the covenant, capable of responding to YHWH's word.
36.5: YHWH's jealous fire against the rest of the nations (Edom specifically and all the nations) who took Israel's land with "wholehearted joy and utter contempt." Edom is singled out (Ezek 25:12-14; 35:1-15; Obad 1) as the paradigm of the nations who exploited Israel's collapse. Edomite expansion into the Negev following 586 BCE is documented archaeologically in the Negev Highland survey data and in later references to "Idumea" as formerly Edomite territory.
36.6: YHWH speaking in jealous wrath against the nations who have become a reproach to Israel — the same jealousy (qinʾah) that drove the judgment against Israel (ch 5:13) is now directed against the taunting nations. The divine jealousy is not petulant but covenantal: YHWH's reputation is bound to Israel's story; when Israel is humiliated, YHWH's name is dishonored among the nations.
36.7: YHWH lifting his hand in oath — the divine oath-gesture (nasati ʾet-yadi, I have raised my hand) is the ANE legal swearing-formula. Raising the hand was the standard gesture for oath-taking in the ancient Near East, documented in both Egyptian and Mesopotamian iconographic sources. YHWH's sworn oath ensures that the nations surrounding Israel will themselves bear shame.
36.8: "But you, mountains of Israel, shoot your branches and yield your fruit to my people Israel" — the botanical restoration imagery (branches, fruit, agricultural abundance) connects to the Deuteronomic blessing vocabulary (Deut 28:1-14). The promise of fruitfulness is the reversal of the covenant curse of barrenness (Lev 26:20; Deut 28:18). The timing — "for they are coming soon" — gives the restoration an eschatological urgency.
36.9: "I am for you" — the divine hostile-presence formula inverted: hinneni ʾeleikhem (behold I am for you/toward you), instead of the judgment formula hinneni ʾaleikha (I am against you). The agricultural activity (turning toward them, being cultivated, being sown) personifies the mountains as a farming operation under divine management. The plowing, sowing, and population-increase are the agricultural covenant-blessing triad.
36.10: Multiplying people on the mountains of Israel — all the house of Israel, all of it — the explicit inclusion of "all of it" anticipates the reunification of Ephraim and Judah announced in ch 37. The cities rebuilt and the waste places inhabited reverse the destruction oracle of ch 6:6 ("cities shall be laid waste and the high places be desolate"). The architectural and demographic restoration are inseparable.
36.11: The multiplication of people and animals on the mountains — a prosperity formula drawn from the Deuteronomic blessing vocabulary (Deut 28:11, "YHWH will make you abound in prosperity in the fruit of your womb and in the fruit of your livestock and in the fruit of your ground"). The declaration "I will settle you as in your former times and will do more good to you than ever before" is YHWH's self-commitment to exceed even the pre-exilic prosperity of the Solomonic period.
36.12: "I will let people walk on you, even my people Israel" — the land is addressed as the place where human beings walk. The land will no longer bereave Israel of children — an unusual promise suggesting that the land itself had been "consuming" its inhabitants through the wars and deportations. This personification of the land as a devourer that YHWH now restrains addresses the community's fear that the land itself had become hostile to human habitation.
36.13: The nations' taunt that the land "devours people and bereaves its nation of children" — a specific accusation the surrounding nations had made about the land of Canaan. The land's reputation as a devourer of its inhabitants appears already in the pre-settlement spy report (Num 13:32, "a land that devours its inhabitants"). YHWH's promise reverses this reputation by ending the land's consuming character.
36.14: "You shall no longer devour people and no longer bereave your nation of children" — the divine promise addressed to the land itself. The grammatical second-person address to the land throughout ch 36 is not merely personification but a genuine oracle directed at the created order: the land has agency in the covenant relationship, capable of receiving divine promises alongside the people.
36.15: The ending of the nations' taunts and the end of the peoples' insults — the social humiliation of exile addressed alongside the agricultural restoration. The land will no longer cause the nation to stumble (the exile had caused many to conclude that YHWH had failed); YHWH declares that the land's covenant relationship with its people is restored.
36.16: A new oracle unit addresses the reason for the exile. The word-event formula resets the prophetic speech. The retrospective analysis of the exile's cause (vv16-21) is the theological foundation for the restoration's motivation (vv22-38).
36.17: The house of Israel living on their land defiling it "like the defilement of a menstruant woman" — the impurity language (ketumat niddah) applies the strongest Levitical purity category to the covenant violation. The land itself had become ritually unclean through Israel's bloodshed and idol-worship, as if defiled by the most serious form of bodily impurity. The Levitical connection between land-defilement and human moral violation (Lev 18:24-28; Num 35:33-34) is the theological principle at work.
36.18: YHWH pouring out his fury on them for the blood they shed in the land and for the idols with which they defiled it — the two-charge formula (blood + idols) that has been the structural basis of Ezekiel's indictment throughout (cf. 22:3-4). The fury poured out corresponds to the cup of wrath imagery of ch 23 and the metallurgical furnace of ch 22.
36.19: The scattering among the nations according to their ways and deeds — the exile as lex talionis. The pattern of Israel's social life was returned to them as the shape of their historical experience. The specific description "according to their ways and according to their deeds I judged them" establishes that the exile was not arbitrary but proportionate to the specific patterns of covenant violation documented in chs 1-24.
36.20: "When they came to the nations, wherever they came, they profaned my holy name, in that people said of them, 'These are the people of YHWH, and yet they had to go out of his land.'" — the theological crisis of the exile: the surrounding nations drew the conclusion that YHWH had failed or been defeated. This theological embarrassment — YHWH's reputation among the nations being damaged by Israel's exile — is the specific concern that drives the restoration. YHWH's honor among the nations is at stake.
36.21: "But I had concern for my holy name, which the house of Israel had profaned among the nations" — the motivation for restoration is explicitly NOT Israel's repentance or merit but YHWH's own reputation (shem qadshi, holy name). This is the most theologically striking move in the book: the restoration is grounded not in Israel's worthiness but in YHWH's self-interest in his own honor among the nations. The Deuteronomy 9:4-5 pattern ("not because of your righteousness") is the background framework.
36.22: "Therefore say to the house of Israel: Thus says Lord YHWH: It is not for your sake that I am about to act, O house of Israel, but for the sake of my holy name." — this explicit denial of Israel's merit as the basis for restoration is theologically radical. ANE restoration theologies typically portrayed the people's repentance or a king's intercession as what moved the deity to restore. Ezekiel's oracle grounds restoration entirely in divine initiative and self-interest, preparing for the new-heart promise that follows: the repentance will be the result, not the cause, of restoration.
36.23: YHWH vindicating the holiness of his name among the nations — the mission-statement of the restoration. The nations will know that YHWH is YHWH when he is vindicated through Israel. The recognition formula here functions at the universal (national) level: the restoration of Israel is an event that communicates YHWH's identity to all nations. This is the Isaianic "servant of YHWH" paradigm (Isa 49:6, "a light to the nations") applied to the restored community.
36.24: The gathering from the nations and bringing into the land — the standard gathering-from-diaspora promise (also 11:17; 20:34, 41-42; 34:13; 37:21). The sequence — first gathering, then cleansing, then new heart — establishes that YHWH acts first; the community's transformation follows the divine action. This is the opposite of a conditional promise where Israel must first change and then YHWH will gather them.
36.25: "I will sprinkle clean water on you and you will be clean; from all your uncleannesses and from all your idols I will cleanse you." — the ritual cleansing by sprinkling (zaraqti ʿaleyhem mayim tehorim) invokes the purification ritual of Numbers 19 (the water of purification made from the ashes of the red heifer, used to cleanse corpse-defilement). The application of this ritual to the entire community (not just individuals with specific contamination) is an unprecedented use of the purification-water concept.
36.26: "A new heart I will give you, and a new spirit I will put within you; I will remove the heart of stone from your flesh and give you a heart of flesh." — the climax of Ezekiel's theology of restoration. The new heart (lev ḥadash) and new spirit (ruach ḥadashah) address the root cause of Israel's failure: not external circumstances but the internal orientation of the community. The heart of stone (lev haʾeven) vs. heart of flesh (lev basar) contrast is the most powerful diagnosis of the human condition in the prophetic literature — the inability to respond to YHWH's word is built into the community's psychological structure. The anticipation of this promise in 11:19 and its parallel in Jer 31:31-34 (the new covenant) establish the new heart/new spirit as the central promise of the post-exilic restoration theology.
36.27: "And I will put my Spirit within you, and cause you to walk in my statutes and be careful to obey my rules." — the divine Spirit (YHWH's own ruach) as the internal enabler of covenant obedience. The Spirit does not merely inspire but causes (ʿasiti, qal perfect of ʿasah, but the causative nuance is important) the obedience — obedience becomes the effect of the Spirit's indwelling rather than a prerequisite for divine favor. This sequence (Spirit first, then obedience) is the theological foundation of Paul's argument in Romans 8:1-4.
36.28: Dwelling in the land they were given — the covenantal land-dwelling formula. The covenant declaration follows: "you shall be my people and I shall be your God" — the shortest and most foundational form of the Sinaitic covenant (Exod 6:7; Lev 26:12; Jer 31:33). The covenant formula functions as the summary statement that the restoration fulfills the original covenantal purpose of the Exodus.
36.29: Salvation from uncleannesses — calling for the grain and multiplying it. The agricultural abundance that follows the internal cleansing and covenant restoration is the Deuteronomic blessing vocabulary (Deut 28:1-14) now grounded in internal transformation rather than external compliance. The agricultural economy of the ancient Near East made harvest abundance the most tangible sign of divine favor; the restoration of grain, wine, and oil (v30) restores the economic foundation of covenant life.
36.30: Multiplying the fruit of trees and produce of the field — the threefold agricultural formula (grain + fruit + harvest) is the Deuteronomic blessing triad (Deut 28:11). The removal of the famine-reproach among the nations is the social vindication that parallels the divine-name vindication of vv22-23: just as YHWH's name is vindicated among the nations by the restoration, the community is vindicated against the famine-reproach that the nations had associated with Israel during the siege and exile.
36.31: "Then you will remember your evil ways and your deeds that were not good, and you will loathe yourselves for your iniquities and your abominations." — the sequence is confirmed: restoration first, then repentance. The community will remember and loathe themselves after the restoration has occurred, not before. This post-restoration self-loathing (also 20:43; 16:63) is the reversed sequence that makes Ezekiel's theology of grace distinctive: YHWH restores the undeserving, and they respond with shame rather than pride.
36.32: "It is not for your sake that I will act" — the explicit anti-merit declaration is repeated from v22. The double declaration (vv22 and 32) creates a literary frame around the restoration promise, ensuring the reader does not misread the restoration as conditional on Israel's merit. The shame and disgrace Israel bears is the appropriate response to undeserved grace rather than a prerequisite that earns it.
36.33: "On the day I cleanse you from all your iniquities, I will cause the cities to be inhabited and the waste places shall be rebuilt" — the synchronization of inner cleansing and external reconstruction. The same day that the new heart is given, the physical land is rebuilt. The inner and outer restoration happen together rather than sequentially, because the land itself participates in the covenant relationship.
36.34: The desolate land tilled instead of being the desolation in the sight of all who passed by — the land's reputation among travelers is restored. Ancient trade routes ran through Canaan; the desolate landscape left by the Babylonian conquest was visible to travelers and traders who passed through, contributing to the international perception of YHWH's failure. The restored land's cultivation reverses this international witness.
36.35: "They will say: This desolate land has become like the garden of Eden" — the Eden comparison is the highest possible standard for land-quality. The garden of Eden as the ideal of abundant, well-watered, flourishing land is the eschatological goal of restoration. The ruined and forsaken and desolate cities made fortified and inhabited — the urban reconstruction is the human correlate of the agricultural restoration.
36.36: The nations round about knowing that YHWH has rebuilt the ruined places and replanted the desolated land — the restoration is a public, internationally visible event. The recognition formula is applied to the surrounding nations who observe the restoration. The witnessing nations' acknowledgment of YHWH's action in restoring Israel completes the "for my name's sake" motivation of v22-23.
36.37: "I, YHWH, have spoken and I will do it" — the divine self-commitment to accomplish what has been declared. This formula occurs throughout Ezekiel's restoration oracles (22:14; 24:14; 37:14) as the absolute guarantee that the prophetic word will be fulfilled. Unlike human speech that can be retracted, YHWH's spoken word carries the force of accomplished fact from the moment it is uttered.
36.38: YHWH responding to Israel's inquiry by multiplying people like a flock, like the flock of Jerusalem at her appointed feasts — the pilgrimage-flock analogy. Jerusalem's three annual pilgrimage feasts (Passover/Unleavened Bread, Weeks/Shavuot, Tabernacles/Sukkot) brought enormous crowds to the city; the population multiplication of restored Israel is compared to the scale of these festival gatherings. The restored land will be filled with people as densely as Jerusalem during the great festivals.

38.1: Standard word-event formula opens the Gog of Magog oracle — the most extensive and most debated oracle in Ezekiel. The entire ch 38-39 unit is a carefully constructed literary composition: ch 38 describes the attack, ch 39 describes the aftermath. The oracle presupposes the restored Israel of chs 34-37 as its context: the attack comes against a people who have been gathered from the nations and are living in security.
38.2: Gog of the land of Magog, chief prince of Meshech and Tubal — the identity of Gog has been debated since antiquity. (1) The most widely held historical identification is with Gyges of Lydia (d. 644 BCE), known in Akkadian sources as Gu-gu of Luddu — a powerful western Anatolian king who sought Assyrian assistance against northern (Scythian/Cimmerian) invaders. (2) The Scythian identification associates Gog with the Scythian (Greek Skythai) migrations through the Levant in the 7th century BCE, documented by Herodotus and by the prophetic tradition of the "northern foe" in Jeremiah 1:14; 4:6; 6:1, 22. (3) More likely, Gog is a symbolic/eschatological figure — the ultimate enemy of YHWH's people at the end of history — drawing on the northern-enemy tradition without requiring a specific historical referent.
38.3: YHWH against Gog — the hostile-presence formula (hinneni ʾeleikha) directed at Gog matches the formula used against the nations in chs 25-32. The oracle is addressed to a figure that YHWH himself summons and turns against Israel (v4), which is the theological key: Gog comes at YHWH's initiative, not merely his own. The attack is YHWH's design, not Gog's independent aggression.
38.4: "I will turn you about and put hooks in your jaws and bring you out with your whole army" — the hooks-in-jaw imagery inverts the normal direction of divine leading: YHWH leads Israel by the hand; he leads Gog by force, as a captive animal. The image of a captured animal with a hook through its jaw appears in Assyrian reliefs depicting the treatment of conquered kings. YHWH uses the same coercive force on the enemy of his people that the Assyrians used on defeated rulers.
38.5: The coalition armies: Persia, Cush, and Put — three geographically distinct regions. Persia (Iran) represents the eastern direction; Cush (Ethiopia/Sudan) the southern; Put (Libya) the western. The three represent the compass directions east, south, and west, with Gog's northern coalition providing the northern element (v6). The four-directional scope of the coalition establishes Gog's attack as a truly universal threat from all directions.
38.6: Gomer and all his hordes, Beth-togarmah from the uttermost parts of the north — Gomer is identified with the Cimmerians (Greek Kimmeroi), a northern steppe people who invaded Anatolia in the 7th century BCE, documented in Assyrian texts. Beth-togarmah is Armenia/southeastern Anatolia. The conjunction of Gomer (Cimmerians) and Meshech/Tubal (peoples of Anatolia and the Black Sea region) in the coalition corresponds to the ANE geography of the "northern peoples" who threatened the civilized world of the ancient Near East.
38.7: "Be ready and keep ready, you and all your company that are assembled around you, and be a guard for them" — YHWH commanding Gog to prepare is the supreme irony of the oracle: the enemy is being prepared for a battle that will become the instrument of YHWH's self-glorification (v23). Gog's military preparation serves YHWH's purposes, not Gog's. The divine sovereignty over the enemy's preparations is the theological claim that the oracle establishes from the outset.
38.8: "After many days you will be mustered; in the latter years you will go against the land." — the temporal setting "after many days" (meʾaḥarit yamim) and "latter years" (beʾaḥarit hashanim) places the Gog oracle explicitly in an eschatological timeframe — later than the restoration oracles of chs 34-37 but still within history. The restored people "gathered from many peoples on the mountains of Israel, which had long been desolate" is the restored community of chs 34-37 as the target of the attack.
38.9: "You will come like a storm — like a cloud covering the land." — the cloud-covering image for overwhelming military force appears throughout the prophetic literature for large invading armies (Isa 8:7-8; Jer 4:13; Joel 2:2). The storm metaphor also connects to the theophanic storm of YHWH's own approach (Ezek 1:4; Nah 1:3); the enemy comes as a pseudo-theophany, imitating but ultimately confronting the genuine divine storm.
38.10: Evil thoughts arising in Gog's mind — the internal deliberation of the enemy revealed to the prophet. The three terms for Gog's mental process ("evil thoughts," "devising an evil scheme") expose the motives behind the military campaign: it is an act of rapacity driven by the perception of easy pickings from an undefended, prosperous people. The disclosure of enemy intentions is a feature of prophetic oracles that establishes YHWH's omniscience — he knows Gog's thoughts before Gog acts on them.
38.11: "I will go up against the land of unwalled villages; I will fall upon the quiet people who dwell securely, all of them dwelling without walls, and having no bars or gates." — the "unwalled villages" (perazot) is the key target-description. Iron Age Canaan had extensive unwalled settlements alongside the fortified cities; after the Babylonian conquest, the rebuilding of fortifications was limited. Gog specifically targets the vulnerability of the restored community — their peace and security (the fulfillment of the new-covenant promise) becomes the occasion for the attack.
38.12: "To seize spoil and carry off plunder, to turn your hand against the waste places that are now inhabited." — the explicitly economic motivation of Gog's campaign. The restored Israel as a uniquely prosperous and uniquely undefended community is the temptation for the attack. The "navel of the earth" (tabur haʾarets) is a cosmological term for the center of the world — also used in Judg 9:37 for a central geographic location. The concept of Jerusalem/Israel as the world's center (omphalos) appears in later Jewish and Christian cosmography (cf. Ezek 5:5, "Jerusalem in the center of the nations").
38.13: Sheba, Dedan, and the merchants of Tarshish asking whether Gog comes to take spoil — the commercial nations observe the military campaign with their own economic interest. Sheba (southwest Arabia) and Dedan (northwest Arabia) were major caravan trade centers; Tarshish (probably Tartessus in Spain) was the furthest western commercial reach of the Phoenician trading network. Their question ("have you come to seize spoil?") is not opposition but curiosity about whether they can benefit from the campaign — they stand aside as observers rather than participants.
38.14: "On that day when my people Israel are dwelling securely, will you not know it?" — YHWH's question to Gog is ironic: the security that provokes Gog's attack is YHWH's own gift to the restored community. Gog attacking the secure Israel is therefore attacking not just a human community but YHWH's own covenantal work. The attack is ultimately an attack on YHWH himself, which is why YHWH responds with the full fury of the theophanic battle in ch 39.
38.15: Gog coming from his place out of the uttermost parts of the north — the direction "uttermost parts of the north" (yarketei tsafon) is the same phrase used for the divine assembly mountain in Ps 48:2 (Zion described as the divine assembly mountain in the north, yarkete tsafon) and in Isa 14:13 for Baal's assembly mountain. The north in Canaanite cosmography was the direction of the divine assembly; Gog comes from the direction associated with the divine realm — a final counterfeit of the divine warrior theme.
38.16: "You will come up against my people Israel, like a cloud covering the land. In the latter days I will bring you against my land" — YHWH explicitly claims the initiative: "I will bring you against my land" (ʾavi'akha ʾal-ʾartsiy). The land is YHWH's (ʾartsiy, my land), not merely Israel's; the attack on it is therefore an attack on YHWH's own property. The purpose — "that the nations may know me when through you, O Gog, I vindicate my holiness before their eyes" — reveals that YHWH brings Gog for self-vindicating purposes.
38.17: "Are you he of whom I spoke in former days by my servants the prophets of Israel?" — YHWH's rhetorical identification of Gog with the northern-enemy tradition of the earlier prophets. This connects the Gog oracle to Jeremiah's "foe from the north" oracles (Jer 1:14; 4:6; 6:1, 22) and possibly to Amos 5:27 and other northern-enemy threats. The Gog oracle is presented as the fulfillment of the entire northern-enemy prophetic tradition — all the prior prophecies of the northern enemy find their ultimate expression in this eschatological assault.
38.18: "On that day when Gog comes against the land of Israel, my wrath will be roused in my anger." — the divine anger at the attack on the restored community. The three-part anger vocabulary (ḥamati + ʾappi + qinʾati, wrath + anger + jealousy) expresses the superlative intensity of YHWH's response. The fire of YHWH's jealousy is the same qinʾah that drove the restoration "for my name's sake" in 36:5 — it is now directed in defense of the restored people.
38.19: "In my jealousy and in my blazing wrath I declare: On that day there shall be a great earthquake in the land of Israel." — the cosmic earthquake as the vehicle of divine judgment. ANE theophany traditions consistently included earthquake as a component of the divine appearance (1 Kgs 19:11-12; Ps 68:7-8; Isa 29:6; Joel 2:10; Amos 1:1; Nah 1:5). The earthquake in this oracle is not merely natural but theophanic — the earth responds to YHWH's presence as it did at Sinai (Exod 19:18).
38.20: The shaking of fish, birds, beasts, creeping things, and all people — the totality of creation responding to the divine earthquake. The comprehensive list of living beings (fish of the sea, birds of the sky, beasts of the field, creeping things, all people on the face of the earth) mirrors the creation-taxonomy of Gen 1. The total-creation response to the divine earthquake is a de-creation moment: the created order is shaken to its foundations by YHWH's direct intervention in history.
38.21: Every sword turned against his brother — the pan-military confusion that destroys the attacking coalition from within. Mutual destruction within the enemy coalition is a recurring motif in YHWH's holy war: Gideon's army of 300 routing the Midianite coalition through their own mutual slaughter (Judg 7:22); Jehoshaphat's victory through the enemy turning on itself (2 Chr 20:23). YHWH fighting for Israel against the nations without Israel needing to fight is the consistent pattern of the holy-war tradition.
38.22: Pestilence, blood, torrential rain, hailstones, fire, and sulfur — the arsenal of covenant-curse weapons directed at Gog. Hail and fire appear in the Egyptian plague narrative (Exod 9:23-24); the sulfur-and-fire vocabulary echoes the destruction of Sodom and Gomorrah (Gen 19:24) and the theophanic battle in Ps 11:6. The pestilence and blood are the siege-weapons used against Jerusalem in chs 5-6 — now directed outward against the enemy of the restored community.
38.23: "I will show my greatness and my holiness and make myself known in the eyes of many nations. Then they will know that I am YHWH." — the ultimate purpose of the Gog oracle and the climax of the entire Ezekiel book's "that they will know that I am YHWH" motif. The defeat of Gog is the final, universal recognition event — not just Israel or the surrounding nations but "many nations" see YHWH's greatness and holiness vindicated in the defeat of the ultimate eschatological enemy.

39.1: Chapter 39 is the second and concluding half of the Gog-Magog oracle that began in ch. 38. The oracle is the longest single prophetic unit in Ezekiel (chs. 38-39, 58 verses) and serves as the climax of the restoration section (chs. 33-39). After ch. 38’s buildup of Gog’s invasion, ch. 39 describes YHWH’s decisive intervention, the aftermath of the battle, and the theological meaning of the event for Israel and the nations. The command “prophesy against Gog” repeats 38:2, creating a structural envelope. The identification of Gog remains debated: a historical nation, an eschatological cipher, or a symbolic representative of all anti-God powers.
39.2: “I will turn you back and drive you forward” (vishuvtecha veshishshetika) — the verb shashah may mean “drive you forward” or “entice you onward” toward Israel. The phrase signals that YHWH is not a passive observer of Gog’s invasion but its author — he is bringing Gog against Israel for his own purposes (38:16: “that the nations may know me”). “From the remotest north” (meyarketei tzafon) — the phrase appears in Isa 14:13 and Ps 48:2 as the location of the divine mountain; it marks Gog’s origin as the far north, the direction associated with the entry of invaders into Israel throughout the prophetic corpus.
39.3: “I will strike your bow from your left hand and make your arrows drop from your right hand” — the military equipment Gog brought for war is instantly rendered useless by direct divine action. The bow and arrow imagery connects to 38:4 (Gog is brought with bows, shields, swords) and deliberately reverses the threat: the weapons that were supposed to destroy Israel are disarmed before a single engagement. The rendering harmless of Gog’s weapons without a conventional battle emphasizes that the victory is entirely YHWH’s, without Israel’s military contribution.
39.4: “You will fall on the mountains of Israel” — the mountains of Israel are the geographic and theological heart of the covenant land. The birds of prey consuming Gog’s army inverts the nations’ expectation: the invaders who came to plunder Israel become the prey. The formula “to birds of prey of every kind and to the wild animals” anticipates the great feast oracle of vv. 17-20 and echoes Deut 28:26 (covenant curse: “your carcasses shall be food for all the birds”) — Gog’s army receives the covenant-curse destined for Israel’s enemies.
39.5: “You will fall in the open field, for I have spoken, declares the Lord GOD” (neum Adonai YHWH). The oracle formula “for I have spoken” grounds the certainty of Gog’s fall not in Israel’s military power or geopolitical advantage but in the irrevocability of YHWH’s word. The “open field” (al-penei hasadeh) — unburied in the open, exposed to scavengers — is the ultimate indignity in the ancient Near Eastern honor culture, the reverse of honorable burial.
39.6: “I will send fire on Magog and on those who dwell securely in the coastlands” — the judgment extends beyond Gog’s army to Magog itself (the homeland) and to the “coastlands” (iyyim), the distant maritime nations. The fire (esh) is the standard OT symbol for divine judgment (Amos 1-2; Isa 29:6; Rev 8:5). The inclusion of the “coastlands” signals that Gog represents a coalition of world powers — the judgment is not merely regional but universal in scope. “They will know that I am the LORD” — the recognition formula marks the theological purpose of even this remote judgment.
39.7: “I will make my holy name known in the midst of my people Israel, and I will not let my holy name be profaned anymore; and the nations will know that I am the LORD, the Holy One in Israel.” The name-theology that governed ch. 20’s historical survey reaches its eschatological resolution: the profanation of YHWH’s name that resulted from Israel’s exile (36:20: “these are YHWH’s people, but they went out of his land”) will never recur. “The Holy One in Israel” (qadosh beyisrael) — YHWH’s holiness is displayed not only among the nations but within the covenant community.
39.8: “Behold, it has come and it has happened, declares the Lord GOD. This is the day of which I have spoken.” The performative declaration — “it has come and it has happened” (hinei vaat uveneta) — gives the oracle a prophetic-perfect quality: the future event is described as already accomplished from the divine perspective. “The day of which I have spoken” connects to the “day of the LORD” tradition in the prophetic corpus (Amos 5:18-20; Joel 2:1; Isa 13:6; Zeph 1:14-15) — Gog’s defeat is the eschatological day YHWH has long announced.
39.9: “The inhabitants of the cities of Israel will go out and make fires with the weapons and burn them” — the seven-year weapon-burning oracle (vv. 9-10) describes the aftermath of Gog’s defeat in terms that reverse the normal flow of plunder. Israel burns the abandoned weapons for fuel rather than chopping wood from forests — indicating both the massive scale of Gog’s army (enough weapons for seven years of fuel) and Israel’s security: they go out from their cities to gather weapons rather than fleeing before enemies. The seven-year duration echoes the seven-month burial period (v. 12) — both numbers suggest completeness and thoroughness of cleansing.
39.10: “They will not need to take wood from the fields or cut any from the forests, for they will fuel their fires with the weapons.” The motif of not needing to work the forests for fuel suggests economic restoration — the land’s normal labor is replaced by the abundance of captured war material. “They will plunder those who plundered them and loot those who looted them” — the reversal formula echoes Isa 14:2 (“they will plunder those who plundered them”) and the covenant-curse reversal of Deut 30:7. The nations that came to despoil Israel become the source of Israel’s wealth.
39.11: “On that day I will give Gog a burial place in Israel — the Valley of the Travelers, east of the sea.” The Valley of the Travelers (gei ha-overim) — also called Hamon-gog (v. 15: “multitude of Gog”) — is east of the Dead Sea (the “sea” in Ezekiel typically refers to the Mediterranean or the Dead Sea depending on context; the location east of a sea suggests the Transjordanian region or the valley east of the Dead Sea). “It will block the path of travelers” — the massive burial site is so large it obstructs the normal transit routes, a dramatic symbol of the scale of Gog’s defeat.
39.12: “The house of Israel will spend seven months burying them in order to cleanse the land.” The seven-month burial period reflects the priestly concern for land-purity: unburied corpses defiled the land under Israelite law (Num 19:11-16; Deut 21:23). The massive undertaking — all of Israel engaged in cleansing the land for seven months — mirrors the Levitical purification procedures scaled to eschatological proportions. The number seven is significant: it corresponds to the sabbatical cycle and suggests complete, thoroughgoing cleansing, not merely partial tidying.
39.13: “All the people of the land will bury them, and it will bring them renown on the day I am glorified, declares the Lord GOD.” The burying of Gog’s dead — normally a humbling task — becomes an occasion for Israel’s honor (shem, renown, name). The contrast with Gog’s dishonor (falling in the open field, v. 5) is sharp: Israel gains renown precisely by performing the act of burying those who came to destroy them. “On the day I am glorified” (beyom hikavdi) — the burial of Gog’s army is simultaneously an act that brings glory to YHWH through Israel’s obedient service.
39.14: The logistics of the burial operation: a permanent detail of men is appointed to search the land for remaining human bones after the initial seven-month mass burial. “After seven months they will begin their search” — the search-and-marker protocol of vv. 14-16 reflects the OT’s priestly concern for complete land-purification. The ongoing work underscores the thoroughness of the cleansing: even individual bones scattered in remote areas must be found and buried, and no one should stumble accidentally upon human remains and incur uncleanness.
39.15: “Whenever any of those traveling through the land sees a human bone, they are to set up a marker beside it until the burial teams have buried it in the Valley of Hamon-gog.” The marker-system reflects the priestly protocol of Num 19:16-19 — anyone who touches a human bone in the open becomes unclean for seven days. The civilian marker-system enlists the general population in the purification effort: ordinary travelers who spot a bone do not bury it themselves (risking uncleanness) but flag it for the burial detail. The systematic, careful thoroughness of the post-battle cleansing emphasizes the complete removal of all that is associated with death and judgment.
39.16: “There will also be a city there called Hamonah.” (gam shem-ir hamonah): the city named Hamonah (from hamon, multitude) commemorates the site of Gog’s burial. The founding of a city named “Multitude” at the site of the mass burial is an ironic monument: the city’s very name preserves the memory of the great multitude defeated by YHWH. The naming of the valley (v. 11: Hamon-gog) and the city (v. 16: Hamonah) creates a permanent memorial — the landscape itself becomes a witness to YHWH’s victory over all nations who rise against Israel.
39.17: Verses 17-20 form the “great sacrifice of YHWH” — a deliberate parody of the covenant sacrificial feast. YHWH invites birds and beasts to a zevach (sacrifice) on the mountains of Israel. The irony is sharp: normally a sacrifice involves animals slaughtered and offered to God; here God’s “sacrifice” is the slaughtered army of Gog, consumed by scavengers. The same imagery appears in Rev 19:17-18 (“the great supper of God”), which directly draws on this passage. The invitation to “every kind of bird and every wild animal” mirrors the invitation formula of a royal feast.
39.18: “You will eat the flesh of the mighty and drink the blood of the earth’s princes — rams, lambs, and goats, bulls, all the fatlings of Bashan.” The military elite of Gog’s coalition are described in terms of sacrificial animals — rams, lambs, goats, bulls. Bashan (in the Golan Heights north of Israel) was famous for its well-fed livestock (Ps 22:12; Amos 4:1). The “fatlings of Bashan” were the finest sacrificial animals; describing Gog’s leaders as “fatlings of Bashan” makes their consumption the consummation of a sacrifice of the highest quality — from YHWH’s perspective, the greatest and most powerful of the nations are merely cattle for slaughter when they oppose him.
39.19: “You will eat fat until you are gorged and drink blood until you are drunk.” The imagery intensifies to a graphic description of total consumption. Blood-drinking was strictly prohibited to Israel (Lev 17:14; Gen 9:4) — only scavenging animals drink blood freely. The reversal: the nations who came bearing weapons against Israel become the nourishment of the natural world’s scavengers. The “sacrifice that I have prepared for you” (mizivchi asher zavachti lachem) establishes YHWH as the host and Gog’s army as the meal — a complete inversion of the nations’ militaristic expectations.
39.20: “You will be filled at my table with horses and chariots, with mighty warriors and every kind of soldier, declares the Lord GOD.” The divine table (shulchani) at which scavengers feast on Gog’s army echoes and inverts the table of the Lord’s Supper: where the NT meal feeds the community with Christ’s body and blood, this eschatological feast feeds the creation with the bodies of those who opposed YHWH. The listing of “horses and chariots, mighty warriors and every kind of soldier” exhausts the categories of military force — no element of Gog’s military power survives.
39.21: “I will display my glory among the nations, and all the nations will see my judgment that I have carried out, and my hand that I have laid on them.” The Gog oracle’s theological purpose — the display of YHWH’s glory among the nations — is now explicitly stated. The recognition formula shifts to a universal scope: “all the nations will see.” YHWH’s “hand” (yad) is the standard OT expression for his powerful historical interventions (the same word used for the exodus: “with a mighty hand”). Gog’s defeat is an event of world-historical significance that announces YHWH’s sovereignty to every nation.
39.22: “From that day forward, the house of Israel will know that I am the LORD their God.” The recognition formula applied specifically to Israel marks the Gog battle as the turning point of Israel’s history: after this day, the sustained unbelief and apostasy that characterized the historical survey of ch. 20 is over. “From that day forward” (miyom hahu vahaleah) is a decisive temporal marker signaling the end of Israel’s pre-restoration ignorance and the beginning of permanent covenant knowledge — the experiential fulfillment of Jer 31:34 (“they shall all know me”).
39.23: “The nations will know that the house of Israel went into exile because of their iniquity — because they were unfaithful to me, I hid my face from them.” The nations’ recognition has two components: (1) Israel’s exile was not YHWH’s defeat but his judgment; (2) YHWH’s restoration of Israel is an act of grace and power, not a capitulation to Babylonian pressure. The phrase “I hid my face from them” (veasater fanai meihem) is a significant OT theological concept: divine absence as judgment (Deut 31:17-18; Isa 54:8; 64:7). The hidden face is the OT equivalent of the experience of divine forsakenness.
39.24: “I dealt with them according to their uncleanness and their transgressions, and I hid my face from them.” The double reference to hiding YHWH’s face (vv. 23-24) frames the exile as a period of divine absence — the opposite of the covenantal blessing of the Aaronic blessing (“YHWH make his face shine on you,” Num 6:25). The exile is the systematic reversal of the covenant blessings: land lost, divine presence withdrawn, nations contemptuous. Verse 24’s formula provides the theological basis for YHWH’s refusal to hide his face permanently (v. 29).
39.25: “Therefore thus says the Lord GOD: Now I will restore the fortunes of Jacob and have compassion on the whole house of Israel, and I will be zealous for my holy name.” The word “therefore” (lachen) here introduces not judgment (as in most of Ezekiel’s “therefore” formulas) but restoration — a significant reversal. “Restore the fortunes” (shavti et-shevut Yaakov): the term shevut (fortunes, captivity) plays on the root shuv (to return) — it is literally “I will return the returning of Jacob.” The jealousy for YHWH’s holy name (vekianti leshem qodshi) returns to the name-theology of vv. 7 and ch. 20: YHWH’s honor motivates restoration.
39.26: “After they have borne their shame and all their faithlessness by which they were unfaithful to me, when they dwell securely in their land with no one to make them afraid.” The “shame” (klimatam) Israel will bear is the same shame of post-restoration self-loathing promised in 20:43 (“you will loathe yourselves”). The bearing of shame is not ongoing condemnation but the natural response of a restored people who understand the gravity of their past unfaithfulness in light of YHWH’s grace. “Dwell securely in their land with no one to make them afraid” — the security formula contrasts with Lev 26:36 (covenant curse: “the sound of a driven leaf shall put them to flight”).
39.27: “When I have brought them back from the peoples and gathered them from the lands of their enemies, and through them have shown myself holy in the sight of many nations.” The gathering from the lands of enemies reverses the scattering of the Deuteronomic curses (Deut 28:64). The instrumental phrase “through them have shown myself holy” (veniqedashti vam) establishes that Israel’s restoration is itself a divine self-revelation event. The gathered, restored, secure Israel demonstrates YHWH’s holiness to the watching nations — making Israel’s very existence and restoration an ongoing witness to YHWH’s character.
39.28: “They will know that I am the LORD their God — I who sent them into exile among the nations, yet I have gathered them to their own land and left none of them behind.” The recognition formula here has a specific theological content: Israel will know that the same YHWH who sent them into exile has gathered them home. The exile and the return are both acts of the same sovereign God — the people will be unable to credit either event to chance, foreign gods, or historical accident. “Left none of them behind” (velo otir od meihem sham) — complete, comprehensive restoration, not a partial remnant return.
39.29: “I will no longer hide my face from them, for I have poured out my Spirit on the house of Israel, declares the Lord GOD.” The chapter’s climactic verse reverses the “hidden face” of vv. 23-24 with a permanent declaration: the face-hiding is over. The ground of this permanent presence is the outpouring of YHWH’s Spirit (shafachti et-ruchi al-beit Yisrael). This is the Ezekielian counterpart to Joel 2:28-29 (“I will pour out my Spirit on all flesh”) and connects to 36:27 (“I will put my Spirit within you”) — the Spirit’s outpouring is the permanent seal of restoration, making YHWH’s presence with Israel irreversible. Peter cites Joel 2:28-29 at Pentecost as the beginning of this fulfillment (Acts 2:17-18).

40.1: The temple vision of chs. 40-48 begins with a precise date formula: “the twenty-fifth year of our exile, at the start of the year, on the tenth of the month, fourteen years after the city had fallen.” Working from the first deportation of 597 BCE, the 25th year = 573/572 BCE. The “start of the year” (berosh hashana) and “tenth of the month” is the tenth of Tishri — Yom Kippur, the Day of Atonement, the holiest day of the Hebrew calendar and the one day when the high priest entered the Most Holy Place. Ezekiel’s vision of the new temple on Yom Kippur of the 25th year of exile (the mid-point of a jubilee cycle) is theologically deliberate. The 25th year is also the beginning of the second half of the 50-year jubilee cycle, when enslaved Israelites were to be freed and land returned (Lev 25).
40.2: “In divine visions he brought me to the land of Israel and set me on a very high mountain, on whose southern slope was something resembling a city.” The visionary transport to Israel (marot Elohim, divine visions) echoes the inaugural vision of ch. 1 and the temple-departure vision of chs. 8-11. The “very high mountain” is Ezekiel’s new Zion — the eschatological mountain that fulfills Isa 2:2 (“the mountain of the house of the LORD shall be established as the highest mountain”). The “something resembling a city” on the southern slope is the visionary temple complex seen from above — a city-sized sacred precinct that Ezekiel is about to explore in meticulous priestly detail.
40.3: “He brought me there, and I saw a man whose appearance was like gleaming bronze (mareh nechoshet), holding a linen measuring cord and a measuring reed, standing in the gateway.” The angelic guide of chs. 40-48 is a divine messenger whose function is to measure and reveal the temple’s dimensions to Ezekiel. The “gleaming bronze” appearance connects to the angelic figure of Dan 10:6 (whose body is like topaz and face like lightning) and Rev 1:15 (the risen Christ whose feet are like refined bronze). The measuring tools — a linen cord for long distances and a measuring reed for shorter measurements — establish the architectural precision of what follows.
40.4: The angelic guide’s command — “look with your eyes, hear with your ears, and fix your attention on everything I am showing you — for you were brought here precisely so that I might show it to you” — is one of the most direct prophetic-commissioning speeches in the OT. The emphatic “precisely so that I might show it to you” signals that every measurement Ezekiel receives is intentional divine disclosure. The command “report all that you see to the house of Israel” makes Ezekiel a precise mediator: he is to transmit the architectural measurements with priestly accuracy, not metaphorical approximation.
40.5: “There was a wall surrounding the outside of the temple complex.” The outer boundary wall establishes the sacred precinct’s perimeter. The “long cubit” (amah vetefach, a standard cubit plus a handbreadth) is the unit of measurement throughout chs. 40-48 — approximately 20.4 inches (52 cm), slightly longer than the ordinary cubit of 17.5 inches (44.5 cm). The outer wall is one measuring rod (six long cubits) thick and one rod high — establishing a massive, imposing boundary that clearly separates the sacred from the common. Ezekiel’s priestly background makes the precise specification of the boundary wall theologically significant: the wall is what makes holiness possible by defining sacred space.
40.6: “He came to the east-facing gate, climbed its steps, and measured the threshold of the gate: one reed deep.” The east gate is the first and primary gate measured — because east is the direction of the divine glory’s approach (43:2: “the glory of God came from the east”). The steps up to the gate threshold establish the progressive ascent of holiness: the outer court is higher than the surrounding land, the inner court higher than the outer (v. 22: seven steps to the outer gate; v. 31: eight steps to the inner). The measurement sequence — outer east gate (vv. 6-16), outer court (vv. 17-19), outer north gate (vv. 20-23), outer south gate (vv. 24-27), inner south gate (vv. 28-31), inner east gate (vv. 32-34), inner north gate (vv. 35-37) — follows a complete circuit of the temple complex.
40.7: “The guardrooms were each one reed long and one reed wide; the spaces between the guardrooms were five cubits.” Each of the outer gates contains six guardrooms (tasot) — three on each side of the gatehouse passageway. The six-chambered gatehouse structure is attested archaeologically at Bronze Age and Iron Age Israelite sites including Megiddo, Hazor, Gezer, and (though smaller) Solomon’s gate at Lachish — Yigael Yadin’s 1958 discovery showed these sites share the same six-chambered plan, which he connected to Solomon’s building program (1 Kings 9:15). The guardrooms provided security posts for controlling access to the sacred precinct.
40.8: “He measured the inner porch of the gate: one reed.” The systematic measurement of each component of the gate complex — threshold (v. 6), guardrooms (v. 7), inner porch (v. 8), entry porch (v. 9), outer wall (v. 5) — reflects priestly precision in defining the sacred architecture. Each gate element has its assigned dimension; nothing is approximate or provisional. The consistent precision across all gates (outer east, outer north, outer south, inner south, inner east, inner north) creates a temple of exact symmetry and order, reflecting the divine character of its designer and the ordered holiness of its occupant.
40.9: “He measured the gate’s entry porch: eight cubits; its doorposts: two cubits. The entry porch faced inward.” The entry porch (ulam hasha-ar) is the vestibule that opens toward the interior of the sacred precinct — “faced inward” (ulam hasha-ar mibayit) establishes the orientation of worshippers as they enter: the gatehouse draws them inward toward the presence of YHWH rather than allowing a view through to the outside. The doorposts (elim) at two cubits width regulate the actual opening through which worshippers pass, establishing the physical boundary of transition into holy space.
40.10: “The guardrooms of the east gate — three on one side, three on the other — were all the same size; the doorposts on either side matched as well.” The uniformity of the guardrooms — identical dimensions on both sides, matching doorposts — creates a structure of perfect bilateral symmetry. In ancient Near Eastern architecture, architectural symmetry expressed cosmic order; in the OT, the tabernacle and temple were also characterized by exact symmetrical arrangements (Exod 26: the two sides of the tabernacle are mirror images). The uniform guardrooms signal that the approach to YHWH’s presence is consistently ordered — not arbitrary or varying.
40.11: “He measured the width of the gate opening: ten cubits, and the gate passage’s total length: thirteen cubits.” The gate opening of ten cubits is wide enough for processions but controlled enough to maintain sacred boundary integrity. The total passage length of thirteen cubits includes the gatehouse interior. These dimensions have been the subject of extensive architectural reconstruction; the most careful analyses (by scholars such as Moshe Greenberg and Daniel Block) place the gatehouse measurements within the parameters of known ancient Near Eastern monumental architecture, though the Ezekielian design is distinctively larger and more elaborate than any known historical temple gate.
40.12: “A low barrier of one cubit stood in front of each guardroom on either side, and each guardroom measured six cubits square.” The low barrier (gvul, one cubit high) in front of each guardroom is a short partition wall that defined the guardroom space without fully closing it off. The six-cubit-square guardroom dimensions create a modular unit that is repeated with perfect consistency throughout the gate complex. The consistent use of modular measurement suggests a design principle of sacred geometry: the entire temple complex is built from repeating proportional units that express mathematical order as an attribute of divine holiness.
40.13: “He measured the full width of the gate, ceiling to ceiling across the opposite guardrooms: twenty-five cubits, door facing door.” The total width of twenty-five cubits (from outside wall to outside wall of the gatehouse) confirms the bilateral symmetry: the gatehouse is exactly 25 cubits wide at its widest point.
