44.28: The priests' inheritance: Vehaytah lahem lenachalah ani nachalatam ('and it shall be their inheritance — I am their inheritance'). This verse is one of the most theologically concentrated in the priestly legislation: the Levitical/Zadokite priests receive no territorial inheritance (nachalah) because YHWH himself is their inheritance. The principle from Num 18:20 ('I am your portion and your inheritance among the sons of Israel') is repeated here for the new order. The material support (first-fruits, sin offerings, guilt offerings, devoted things) flows from YHWH's own provision to them through the people's worship.
44.29: The priestly food portions: Hamminchah vehechatat vehe'asham hemah yo'khelu ('the grain offering, the sin offering, and the guilt offering — they shall eat them'). The three categories of holy-of-holies offerings that the priests eat in the sacred precinct correspond to Lev 6:17-18, 6:25-29, 7:6-7. Vekhol-cherem beYisrael lahem yihyeh ('and every devoted thing in Israel shall be theirs'): the cherem property — things irrevocably dedicated to YHWH — forms part of the priestly economic base (Num 18:14; Lev 27:21, 28).
44.30: First-fruits and first-born: Vere'shit kol-bikkurei kol vekol terumot mikol ('the first of all first-fruits of everything and every contribution from everything'). The complete system of first-fruits (Exod 23:19; Num 18:12-13; Deut 18:4) and contributions (terumot) flows to the priesthood. The theological logic: the priest stands at the interface between YHWH and Israel; first-fruits acknowledge YHWH's ownership of the land's produce, and the priests who represent YHWH receive this acknowledgment. Lema'an havuah berakhah el-beitecha ('so that a blessing may rest on your house'): the first-fruits offering activates blessing on the household — the material exchange participates in covenantal blessing-logic.
44.31: The priestly food prohibition: Kol-nevelah uterefah min-ha'of umin-habeheimah lo yo'khelu hakohanim ('the priests shall not eat any bird or beast that died of itself or was torn'). The final priestly food restriction: nebelah (died naturally/improperly) and terefah (torn by predators) are excluded regardless of species. This applies to birds and animals (Lev 22:8 for priests; Lev 11:39-40 and Deut 14:21 for all Israelites — though ordinary people may give nebelah to foreigners). The priests as holy persons must eat food that was properly slaughtered and handled — their table is an extension of the altar's purity standards.

45.1: The terumah (sacred portion) for the temple: Uvechalleqchem et-ha'aretz benachalah tarimu terumah lADONAI ('when you apportion the land as an inheritance, you shall offer a contribution to YHWH'). The new allocation begins with the sacred district at the land's center — the arrangement is theocentric: YHWH's domain at the center, then the priestly portion, then the Levitical portion, then the city, with the nasi's territory flanking both sides (45:7). The terumah concept (lifted portion, elevated offering) from Exod 25:2-3 becomes here a geographic category — a lifted-out zone of sacred territory.
45.2: The holy precinct dimensions: 500 by 500 square with 50 cubits of open space around it. The temple precinct (25,000 by 25,000 cubits holy area) contains within it a 500-cubit-square core for the sanctuary itself. The open space (miqreh) around the sanctuary functions as a buffer — keeping the most sacred structure physically separated from the surrounding territory. This spatial separation echoes the concentric holiness zones of the tabernacle: court, holy place, holy of holies — here translated into a geographic/urban planning principle.
45.3: The holy portion for the sanctuary: From the 25,000 cubit measure (east-west) the portion belonging to the sanctuary (qodesh qodashim) — the innermost district. The dimensions create nested zones of decreasing holiness radiating outward from the sanctuary: the innermost square → the priestly housing area → the Levitical territory → the city → the agricultural/nasi's land. This zoning plan makes visible what the temple's inner architecture already expressed: sacred reality diminishes in intensity as you move away from its source.
45.4: The priestly portion: The 25,000 by 10,000 cubit strip within the holy portion belongs to the priests who minister in the sanctuary. They will use it for houses and as a sacred place for the sanctuary (liqodesh lamiqdash). The priests' residential allocation is tied to the sanctuary — their housing exists within the sacred zone, reflecting the principle that priestly life is an extension of temple service. This contrasts with the monarchic period when priestly families could reside anywhere; the new order grounds them geographically at the sanctuary.
45.5: The Levitical portion: An equal 25,000 by 10,000 cubit strip for the Levites who serve in the house (mesharetey habayit). Twenty chambers (lishkot) as their possession. The Levites receive the same geographic extent as the priests but on the outer ring — their spatial position reflects their functional position: supporting the priestly service from a step removed. The twenty chambers (esrim lishkot) presumably house the Levitical families in communal clusters appropriate to their work-bands.
45.6: The city portion: 5,000 cubits wide and 25,000 cubits long, beside the holy terumah — this belongs to the whole house of Israel. The city territory is the broadest category: unlike the priestly and Levitical strips which belong to specific groups, the city's supporting hinterland belongs to "the whole house of Israel" (lechol beit Yisrael). This democratic allocation ensures that the city (which includes the temple) is the possession of the entire covenant community, not of a privileged class. The city's inclusivity counters the priestly-only exclusivity of the inner zones.
45.7: The nasi's (prince's) land: Velanasi mizzo uvemizo ('and to the prince, on either side'): the nasi's land flanks the sacred terumah and the city territory on both east and west sides, stretching to the eastern and western sea coasts. This allocation ensures the nasi is never landlocked or land-poor — he has territory on both sides of the central sacred district. The flanking position means the prince surrounds the sacred center without encroaching on it, representing his role as protector and patron of the sacred space without being its master.
45.8: The land as the prince's to prevent oppression: La'aretz yihyeh lo lesegulah be'Yisrael ('it shall be his land as a possession in Israel'). The explicit purpose: the princes shall no longer oppress my people, and the land shall be given to the house of Israel by their tribes. The Ezekiel vision emerged from the context of monarchic land-grabbing (1 Kgs 21 — Naboth's vineyard being the paradigmatic case; Mic 2:1-2; Isa 5:8). The nasi's guaranteed territorial portion eliminates the economic pressure that drove monarchic land seizures: he already has enough.
45.9: Direct address to the princes: "Enough!": Koh amar Adonai YHWH rav lakhem nesiey Yisrael ('thus says the Lord YHWH: enough, princes of Israel!'). The divine rebuke names two specific crimes: chamas umeshod ('violence and oppression/plunder'). Hasevu umishpat utzedeqah asu ('remove your violence and oppression and execute justice and righteousness'). The prophetic tradition's central social-justice demand is repeated here: justice and righteousness (mishpat utzedeqah) are the standing requirements for leadership. Isaiah 1:17; Amos 5:24; Jer 22:3 — the same pair appears throughout the prophetic corpus as the non-negotiable standard for covenant leaders.
45.10: Standardized weights and measures: Mozney tzedeq ve'eifat tzedeq uvat tzedeq yihyeh lakhem ('just balances, just ephah, and just bath shall you have'). The demand for standardized weights and measures is a covenant-justice requirement rooted in Lev 19:35-36, Deut 25:13-15, and Prov 11:1, 16:11, 20:10, 20:23. Moznei tzedeq (just balances): the balance-scale was the primary commercial measurement tool in the ancient Near East; inaccurate scales were a systematic mechanism for economic exploitation, benefiting sellers who used underweight scales and buyers who used overweight scales depending on context.
45.11: Ephah and bath: unified capacity measures: Ha'eifah vehabet tochen echad yihyeh ('the ephah and the bath shall be the same capacity'). The ephah was the dry measure for grain; the bath was the liquid measure for oil and wine. Equating them establishes a unified system: both = 1/10 of a homer/kor. The archaeological record shows considerable variation in actual vessel capacities labeled "bath" — finds from excavations at Tell Beit Mirsim and Lachish show bath-jar fragments with wide size variation, confirming that the standardization called for here addressed a real commercial problem. A homer/kor of ten ephahs/baths creates a clean decimal system.
45.12: Shekel and maneh standards: Twenty gerahs = one shekel; sixty shekels = one maneh. The shekel standard (20 gerahs) matches Exod 30:13; Lev 27:25; Num 3:47. The maneh at 60 shekels differs from some other ancient Near Eastern maneh standards (the Babylonian maneh = 60 shekels, consistent with Ezekiel's Babylonian context). The fact that Ezekiel specifies 60 shekels per maneh may reflect a correction of Judahite practice that had drifted from the Babylonian standard, or an alignment with the exilic community's commercial context in Babylon.
45.13: The contribution rates: Zot ha-terumah asher tarimu ('this is the contribution you shall offer'). 1/6 of an ephah from each homer of wheat; 1/6 of an ephah from each homer of barley. The contribution rates are modest fractions — not onerous — establishing a regular transfer from agricultural production to the sanctuary economy. The specific rate (1/60 for wheat: 0.1 ephah = 1/60 of a homer if homer=6 ephahs, or 1/6 if homer=1 ephah) places this within the tithe tradition while specifying which portion goes directly to the sacred terumah as opposed to other tithes.
45.14: Oil contribution: The oil contribution: 1/10 of a bath from each kor (since 10 baths = 1 kor/homer). The bath of oil gives 1/10 of a kor, making the proportion consistent. Oil (shemen) was one of the principal agricultural products of Canaan/Israel alongside grain and wine (Deut 7:13; 11:14; Hos 2:8-9). Its inclusion in the contribution system reflects both its economic significance and its liturgical centrality — the lamp-oil, anointing oil, and cereal-offering oil were all critical to temple functioning.
45.15: Sheep contribution for offerings: One sheep from every two hundred from the watered pastures of Israel (mimashqeh Yisrael). This flock-contribution supports the grain offerings, burnt offerings, and peace offerings that effect atonement. Lechapper aleichem ('to make atonement for you'): the explicit purpose of the contribution system is atonement-maintenance. The entire economic structure of contributions, weights, and measures is designed not for institutional self-enrichment but for the continuous maintenance of the atonement mechanisms that sustain Israel's covenantal relationship with YHWH.
45.16: All the people's contribution: Kol-ha'am ha'aretz yihyu el-haterumah hazot ('all the people of the land shall be for this contribution'). The contribution system is not optional or merely aspirational — all the people of the land participate. The nasi is the manager of this collective contribution system, not merely a contributor himself. He receives (collects from the people) and provides (gives to the sanctuary) — the financial intermediary between the people and the temple economy. This establishes the nasi's role as the sanctuary's fiscal patron without making him its owner.
45.17: The nasi's liturgical provision: Ve'al-hanasi yihyeh haolot vehaminchah vehanasekh bamoadim uvechodashim uveshabbatot ('and upon the prince shall be the burnt offerings, grain offerings, and drink offerings, at feasts, new moons, and Sabbaths'). The nasi bears responsibility for the public worship cycle. This is the liturgical corollary of his territorial provision: just as his land allocation ensures he cannot be economically coercive (v.8), his fiscal responsibility for the public cult ensures the cult is adequately funded. The three-tier calendar (feasts, new moons, Sabbaths) represents the full rhythm of Israel's worship year.
45.18: First month, first day: sanctuary purification: Barishon be'echad lachodesh tiqach par ben-baqar tamim vechateta et-hamiqdash ('in the first month, on the first day of the month, you shall take a bull without blemish and purify the sanctuary'). The yearly calendar of purification begins here: the New Year purification of the sanctuary on 1 Nisan (the spring New Year). This is analogous to but not identical with the Day of Atonement purification of Lev 16 (10 Tishri). Ezekiel's calendar separates the priestly purity calendar from the Levitical 7th-month calendar, using a first-month purification for the temple building.
45.19: Blood on the doorposts and altar: The priest takes some of the blood of the purification offering and applies it to the doorposts of the temple, the four corners of the ledge of the altar, and the doorposts of the inner court gate. The blood-smearing ritual echoes the Passover blood on doorposts (Exod 12:7, 22-23) and the altar-consecration blood of Exod 29:12 and Lev 8:15. In both cases, blood marks a threshold as YHWH's territory — the purification blood restores the sacred markers of ownership and consecration that may have become "dulled" through the previous year's ritual use.
45.20: The seventh day of the first month: The same purification is performed on the 7th day of the first month, for anyone who sinned inadvertently or through ignorance (bishgagah umin-peti). The inadvertent-sin category (shegagah) from Num 15:22-29 requires atonement for both individual and communal unintentional violations. The repetition of the sanctuary purification on the 7th day creates a week-long purification period that cleanses both the structure (1st day) and the accumulated inadvertent offenses of the community (7th day). The sanctuary is being reset to a pure starting state for the year.
45.21: Passover: 14th of the first month: Barishon bearba'ah asar yom lachodesh yihyeh lakhem hapesach chag shiv'at yamim matzot ye'achel ('in the first month, on the fourteenth day, you shall have the Passover, a feast of seven days, unleavened bread shall be eaten'). Passover remains the foundational commemorative feast — the exodus from Egypt. The seven days of unleavened bread correspond to Exod 12:14-20; Lev 23:6-8; Num 28:17-25. The Passover occupies the same calendar position as in the Pentateuch but here is described as a gift of the nasi to the congregation — it is incorporated into the prince's liturgical responsibility (v.17).
45.22: Passover sin offering: bull for the prince: On the Passover day, the nasi shall provide a bull as a sin offering for himself and all the people of the land (vela'am ha'aretz par chatat). The nasi's sin offering is the first sacrifice of the Passover — acknowledging that even the prince stands before YHWH in need of atonement. This leveling function of the sacrifice system is consistent throughout the Torah's sacrificial code: the anointed priest (Lev 4:3), the ruler (Lev 4:22), and the common person (Lev 4:27) all bring sin offerings, with the higher role bringing the more costly animal.
45.23: The seven-day Passover burnt offering: Seven bulls and seven rams daily for seven days (shiv'at yamim ya'aseh olah lADONAI shiv'at parim veshiv'at eilim temimim liom). This seven-fold offering structure mirrors the Num 28:19 Passover offering schedule. The sin offering is one male goat daily. The seven-day intensity of burnt offerings during Passover reflects the magnitude of the exodus-event being commemorated: the nation's founding liberation from Egypt. The sevenfold repetition (7 bulls + 7 rams × 7 days) creates an arithmetic expression of completeness-within-completeness.
45.24: Grain and oil offerings accompanying the Passover: An ephah of grain offering per bull and ram, and a hin of oil per ephah. The accompaniment of grain and oil with burnt offerings follows the standard formula of Num 15:1-12 where each animal offering is attended by specific grain and liquid measure proportions. The hin of oil per ephah of grain is a generous measure — approximately 1:1 by volume — ensuring substantial quantities of oil are consecrated in the grain offerings. These proportions ground the temple economy in the agricultural reality of grain and olive cultivation in the land.
45.25: Feast of Tabernacles: 7th month, 15th day: Bashvi'i bachamisha asar yom lachodesh bachag ya'aseh ka'elleh shiv'at yamim ('in the seventh month, on the fifteenth day of the month, at the feast, shall he do like these things for seven days'). Sukkot (Tabernacles/Booths) receives the same seven-day burnt offering and sin offering schedule as Passover — a symmetry that brackets the agricultural year: Passover (1 Nisan, spring barley/grain beginning) and Tabernacles (15 Tishri, fall harvest completion). The two feasts frame the agricultural year with its bookend sacrifices, sanctifying the entire agricultural cycle within the covenant calendar.

46.1: The inner east gate on Sabbath and New Moon: Sha'ar hechatzer hapniymit hapaneh qadim yihyeh sagur sheshet yemey hama'aseh ('the gate of the inner court facing east shall be shut the six working days'). The same east gate that was discussed in ch44 in relation to the prince's access is here given its regular weekly schedule. On Sabbaths and New Moons it is opened (uveyom haShabbat yipatach uveyom hachodesh yipatach) — the elevated sacred days receive elevated architectural access. The gate's weekly opening cycle creates a physical expression of the sacred calendar: the gate itself marks time by opening and closing with the rhythm of holy days.
46.2: The prince's Sabbath and New Moon ritual: Uvah hanasi derek ulam shaar mihutz ('the prince shall enter by way of the porch of the gate from outside'): the nasi enters through the east inner gate porch, stands by the gate doorpost, watches the priests offer his burnt offering and peace offering, and then prostrates himself (vehishtachavah al-miftan hasha'ar veyatza). The specific exit route — the way he came — is specified. This ritual entry and exit pattern is significant: the nasi participates in the Sabbath worship through observation and prostration at the threshold, the maximum access permitted him short of entering the inner court.
46.3: The congregation worships at the outer gate: The people of the land (am ha'aretz) prostrate themselves at the entrance to this gate before YHWH on Sabbaths and new moons. The layered worship positions are architecturally enacted: the priests are inside offering at the altar, the prince is at the inner gate porch, the congregation is at the outer gate entrance — each group is as close to YHWH as their status permits. This is a spatial liturgy: the worshipping community is arranged in concentric rings of proximity that express the holiness gradient in real time.
46.4: Sabbath burnt offering: six lambs and a ram: Veha'olah asher-yaqriv hanasi lADONAI beyom haShabbat shishah kevasim temimim ve'ayil tamim. The Sabbath offering of six lambs and one ram from the nasi differs from the Num 28:9-10 prescription (two lambs for the Sabbath). The heightened offering in Ezekiel reflects either a more elaborate future liturgy or the increased responsibility of the nasi as the sanctuary's fiscal patron. The Sabbath is the temporal center of the weekly cycle just as the inner sanctuary is the spatial center of the temple complex — appropriate that the prince's highest weekly sacrifice occurs on the Sabbath.
46.5: Grain offering proportions: An ephah per ram and whatever he can afford (matnat yado) per lamb, and a hin of oil per ephah. The flexible provision for the lamb grain offering (matnat yado = 'the gift of his hand' = as much as he can give) introduces a pastoral accommodation: the lamb offering's grain accompaniment scales with capacity. This is not a reduction of the standard but a recognition that multiplied lamb offerings might require flexible grain quantities. The ram's grain offering remains fixed at one ephah — only the more numerous lamb portion is made flexible.
46.6: New Moon offering: a bull, six lambs, and a ram: The New Moon (rosh chodesh) offering adds a bull to the Sabbath formula, making the New Moon the higher of the two regular cycles. This matches the Num 28:11-15 principle that the New Moon offering exceeds the Sabbath offering in number. The bull represents a step up from the Sabbath lamb-and-ram combination, reflecting the New Moon's status as a monthly community feast (1 Sam 20:5, 18; Amos 8:5; Isa 1:13-14). The monthly rhythm of the new moon created a punctuation mark in the calendar requiring a more generous public offering.
46.7: New Moon grain offerings: An ephah for the bull, an ephah for the ram, whatever he can afford for the lambs, and a hin of oil per ephah. The grain proportions replicate the Passover grain formula (45:24) — an ephah per major animal (bull/ram) and flexible for lambs. The consistent use of the hin of oil per ephah creates a standard oil-to-grain ratio across all the major offering occasions. This standardization prevents arbitrary escalation or reduction of the offering components — the prince cannot substitute different ratios even if he provides more expensive base animals.
46.8: The prince's entry and exit pattern: Uvevo'o hanasi derek ulam hasha'ar yavo uvederekh yetze ('when the prince enters, he shall enter by the way of the gate porch, and by the same way he shall go out'). The reciprocal entry-exit pattern for ordinary days. The gate-protocol has a theological logic: entering and exiting by the same route prevents the prince from "cutting through" the sacred space — each traversal is intentional and controlled. The prince's movements through the sanctuary are liturgical acts, not casual transits.
46.9: The congregation's entrance and exit pattern: Uvevo bam ha'aretz lifney YHWH bamoadim haba derech sha'ar tzafon lehishtachavot yetze derech sha'ar negev ('when the people of the land come before YHWH at the appointed feasts, whoever enters by the north gate to worship shall go out by the south gate'). The congregation uses a different traffic flow: north-in, south-out (or vice versa). This prevents crowding and creates an orderly flow. The requirement that no one returns by the same entrance gate applies to the entire congregation: the one-way flow ensures that the sacred space is traversed in a directed manner — the congregation moves through the sacred zone rather than oscillating in it.
46.10: The prince moves with the people: Vehanasi betocham bevo'am yavo uvetseatam yetze'u ('and the prince among them — when they enter he shall enter, and when they go out he shall go out'). The nasi does not have a private worship schedule separate from the congregation: he enters and exits with the people, sharing their liturgical movement. This is the democratic constraint on the nasi's elevated liturgical status: he has special gate access and special offering responsibilities, but he moves with his people rather than independently of them. The prince's solidarity with the congregation is architecturally enforced.
46.11: Feast and festival grain offerings: At feasts and appointed times, the grain offering shall be an ephah per bull and per ram, and whatever he can afford per lamb, and a hin of oil per ephah. This verse establishes the consistency of the offering formula across all the major occasions: Passover (45:24), New Moon (46:7), Feast of Tabernacles (45:25), and now general feasts — all use the same ephah-per-large-animal and hin-per-ephah formula. The consistency is the point: the sacred calendar operates with uniform mathematical proportions, creating predictability and preventing ad-hoc variation.
46.12: Freewill offerings: the east gate opened: Vechi ya'aseh hanasi nedavah olah o shelamim nedavah lADONAI ('and when the prince offers a freewill offering (nedavah), whether a burnt offering or a peace offering, as a freewill offering to YHWH'). The nedavah (freewill/spontaneous offering) from Lev 22:18-25 is here given a special architectural provision: the east inner gate is opened for it, as on Sabbath and New Moon. The spontaneous devotion of the prince triggers the same gate-opening as the fixed calendar — YHWH receives individual devotion with the same welcome as liturgical obligation. The gate is opened and shut after the prince exits, maintaining its normally-closed status.
46.13: The daily morning lamb: Keves ben-shenato tamim ya'aseh olah layom tamid ('a yearling lamb without blemish shall be offered as a burnt offering to YHWH daily — the tamid'). The tamid (continual/regular) offering of one lamb each morning continues from Exod 29:38-42 and Num 28:3-8, which prescribed two lambs daily (morning and evening). Ezekiel's restoration version has only the morning lamb with no evening companion — a simplification or possibly a transitional arrangement. The morning lamb anchors every day in sacrificial worship: the first act of each day is a burnt offering to YHWH.
46.14: Daily grain and oil for the tamid: 1/6 of an ephah of grain and 1/3 of a hin of oil to moisten the flour — a perpetual grain offering, always (chok olam tamid). The perpetual nature of the daily offering system is emphasized by the doubled absolute: chok olam (eternal statute) and tamid (continual). The consistency of the offering formula ensures that the daily sacrifice is never a matter of mood or circumstance — it is an institutional obligation as permanent as the covenant itself. The grain and oil proportions for the morning tamid are specified to prevent arbitrary variation.
46.15: Daily lamb, grain, and oil — morning by morning: Ya'asu et-hakeves veha-minchah veha-shemen baboqer baboqer olat tamid ('they shall prepare the lamb, the grain offering, and the oil morning by morning as a perpetual burnt offering'). The threefold baboqer baboqer (morning by morning) echoes the Exodus manna-gathering formula (baboqer baboqer, Exod 16:21) — each new day begins with YHWH's provision meeting Israel's need and Israel's offering meeting YHWH's worship. The daily sacrificial rhythm is Israel's covenant heartbeat: as long as the tamid burns, the covenant is active.
46.16: The prince's inheritance law: sons: Ki yiten hanasi mattanah le'achad mibanav nachalatov hi livanaiv tihyeh ('if the prince gives a gift from his inheritance to one of his sons, it shall be his — it belongs to his sons as a hereditary possession'). The nasi may permanently alienate portions of his land allocation to his sons. This is a hereditary gift within the family — consistent with the normal inheritance laws. The permanent transfer to sons is the only form of permanent alienation permitted; all other transfers are temporary (reverts at jubilee). The son-permanent/servant-temporary distinction maintains the tribal integrity of land distribution across generations.
46.17: Gifts to servants revert at the Jubilee: Ve'im yiten mattanah me'nachalato le'achad me'avadav ('but if he gives a gift from his inheritance to one of his servants'): the servant's gift is temporary, reverting in the year of Jubilee. Halibanaiv tihyeh nachalatam hi ('for it is their inheritance — it belongs to his sons'). The Jubilee principle from Lev 25:8-17 governs the prince's gift-giving: he cannot permanently impoverish the tribal allotments by giving them to non-tribal members. The annual accumulation that led to the economic disparities condemned in Isa 5:8 ('woe to those who join house to house') is structurally prevented by the Jubilee reversion.
46.18: The prince shall not take the people's inheritance: Velo yiqach hanasi menahalt ha'am lehodiham me'achutzatam ('the prince shall not take any part of the inheritance of the people, pushing them out of their property'). This verse explicitly prohibits the Naboth's Vineyard scenario (1 Kgs 21) — royal land acquisition by coercion. The nasi may only give from his own allotted territory, not from the people's tribal holdings. Lemen lo yafutzu ammi ish me'achutzato ('so that my people shall not be scattered each from his property'): the connection between land tenure and communal stability is explicit — dispossession leads to dispersal, which is the covenant-curse of exile. The restoration order structurally prevents the conditions that led to the pre-exilic social catastrophe.
46.19: Ezekiel is led by the gate entrance to the holy chambers of the priests (lishkot haqodesh asher lak-kohanim) facing north. In the westernmost part, there is a place at the two ends. The priestly cooking areas are at the extreme west end of the two side chambers — physically separated from the sacrificial preparation areas to prevent inadvertent transfer of holiness from guilt/sin offerings to other foods. The spatial organization of cooking zones reflects the theological organization of holiness categories: certain offering types are too charged to be handled in proximity to general food preparation.
46.20: Where priests cook the guilt and sin offerings: Zeh hamaqom asher yevashelu sham hakohanim et-he'asham ve'et-hachatat ('this is the place where the priests shall boil the guilt offering and the sin offering'). The inner cooking courts are the only places where the asham (guilt offering) and chatat (sin offering) may be cooked — both are holy-of-holies offerings (Lev 6:25-29; 7:6-7) that cannot be brought into the outer court lest they consecrate the people there. The principle: lebilti hotzia el-hechatzer hachutzah liqaddesh et-ha'am ('so as not to bring them out to the outer court and consecrate the people'). Holy-of-holies holiness is contagious — it would "charge" anyone who touches the place where these offerings were cooked.
46.21: The outer court has four corner courts, each 40 by 30 cubits, and they are of equal size — the four corners of the outer court. The symmetric placement of the four cooking courts at the corners creates a perfectly balanced outer court layout. This architectural symmetry is itself a theological statement: the provision for ordinary people's cooking of their peace offerings (as opposed to the inner holy-of-holies offerings) is given equal weight at all four compass points. No direction of the outer court is prioritized over another for ordinary communal meal-preparation.
46.22: The four corner courts are enclosed courts, 40 by 30 each. A row of masonry (tira'ot) is built around the inner edge of each. These small enclosed spaces function as roofed or semi-roofed kitchens — the architectural detail of the surrounding masonry suggests permanent cooking facilities rather than temporary arrangements. The excavation of Iron Age installations at temple sites in the Levant (Tel Arad, Tel Dan) reveals permanent cooking and offering-preparation infrastructure as a standard feature of Israelite cultic sites, confirming the practical necessity of what Ezekiel describes.
46.23: The hearths under the surrounding ledge: Umesharetin tachat hamisgeret saviv saviv ('and places for boiling beneath the rows around'). The cooking hearths are built beneath a surrounding ledge — presumably a protective overhang that keeps weather out while allowing smoke to escape. The technical detail of permanent fixed hearths reflects the practical reality that large-scale sacrificial preparation requires substantial, permanent cooking infrastructure. The daily tamid sacrifice plus festival offerings plus peace offerings from thousands of worshippers creates an enormous food-preparation demand that the temple's outer court must accommodate.
46.24: The cooking courts for the people's offerings: Va'yomer elay elleh beit hamevashellim asher yevashelu sham mesharetey habayit et-zevach ha'am ('and he said to me: these are the boiling houses where the temple ministers shall boil the sacrifice of the people'). The final verse of ch46 closes the architectural tour with a practical detail: the outer court cooking areas serve the people's peace offerings, which are eaten by the worshipper and their family (Lev 7:11-17). The restoration temple provides not just a place for priests to offer and YHWH to dwell, but a full communal meal infrastructure — the sacred feast that bonds Israel to YHWH and to each other is physically built into the temple's ground plan.

47.1: The temple river vision begins with water flowing from below the south side of the temple threshold (miftan habbayit). In ANE cosmology, the temple was built at the center of the world over the apsu — the primordial freshwater ocean beneath the earth from which rivers spring. Babylon's Esagila temple was located over the symbolic apsu; the cosmic water flowing from the temple's foundation was an architectural and theological statement. Jerusalem's temple on Mount Moriah had a geographic precedent: the Gihon spring (the city's primary water source) flows from below the eastern hill of Jerusalem. Ezekiel's vision supernaturalizes this existing reality: the water that trickled from the Gihon becomes a cosmic river. The eastward direction is topographically accurate — the Kidron valley runs east-southeast from Jerusalem toward the Jordan rift and the Dead Sea.
47.2: The angel-guide leads Ezekiel out through the north gate (where the waters would not yet be visible) and around the outside to the east gate — positioning him as an observer of the water emerging from the south side. The north-gate exit and east-gate observation point are part of Ezekiel's characteristic approach: the vision's features are revealed incrementally, as a tour of the eschatological temple complex. “Water trickling out from the south side” (mayim mefakkim min-hakkathef hayemanit) — a trickle at the source, not yet impressive in volume, establishes the contrast with what follows. The miracle is not in the starting volume but in the unexplained growth.
47.3: The measuring man's 1,000-cubit intervals (approximately 500 meters each) give the water-depth survey a systematic, careful structure. At the first measurement Ezekiel is ankle-deep — the water is real and physical, not a vision-illusion. A cubit is roughly 18 inches / 45 cm; ankle-deep in a 1,000-cubit-wide channel is already substantial. The measuring line (qav hamidah) is the same instrument used throughout chs. 40-43 for the temple measurements — the eschatological order is precisely measured, not vague or approximate. The precision contrasts with the chaotic disorder of the exile and the imprecise vagueness of merely human hope.
47.4: The water doubles in depth at each station — ankle to knee to waist — without any visible tributaries or additional sources. Natural rivers deepen as tributaries join them; this river deepens from the sacred source alone. The parallel in the NT is the miraculous multiplication of resources from a divine source: the feeding of 5,000 begins with what a child can carry and becomes more than sufficient for thousands (John 6:9-13). The eschatological water follows the same logic of divine surplus: YHWH's provision does not merely match need — it overflows all natural limits.
47.5: “A river I could not cross, for the water had risen; it was deep enough to swim in, a river that could not be forded” (nahal asher lo-uchal la-avor ki-gavu hamayim mey-sivah nahal asher lo-ye-aver) — impassable depth. The verb “could not cross” signals that the river now exceeds human control and navigation: one can no longer wade through it by one's own strength but must swim — that is, surrender to the current. The theological implication has been recognized throughout the Christian tradition: the river of divine grace overflows human management. John's “rivers of living water” (John 7:38) and Revelation's “river of the water of life” (Rev 22:1) cite this exact verse as their OT source.
47.6: “Son of man, have you seen this?” — the angel's question invites Ezekiel to register the significance of what he has witnessed. The same rhetorical invitation appears in the vision of the dry bones (37:3: “can these bones live?”) and in Zechariah's visions (Zech 4:2: “what do you see?”). The divine guide does not lecture but questions — drawing the prophet into active contemplation rather than passive reception. “He led me back to the bank of the river” — returning to the observation point for the next phase of the vision: the trees and the sea.
47.7: “A great many trees on both sides” (etz rav meod al-sefat hanahal mizzo umizo) — the trees lining the river's banks are the first preparation for v.12's fruit-and-healing description. In Genesis 2:9-10, a river flowed out of Eden and the tree of life stood in the garden; here the river flows out of the eschatological temple and the trees stand on both banks. The river-and-trees combination is the Eden-garden pattern: where YHWH's river flows, the original paradise-garden returns. The ancient Israelite audience would have recognized this as the restoration of what was lost at the Fall.
47.8: The river flows to the eastern region, down to the Arabah (the Jordan rift valley south of the Dead Sea), and into the sea — the Dead Sea (yam haqadmoni, the eastern sea). “When they flow into the sea, the water there will become fresh” (venitrefu hamayim, literally will be healed) — the same verb used for healing wounds and diseases. The Dead Sea (averaging 33% salinity, approximately 10 times saltier than the ocean) is the most lifeless large body of water on earth. No fish live in it; its shores are salt formations; its name in Hebrew (Yam haMelach, Sea of Salt) reflects its absolute barrenness. That YHWH's river will heal it is the paradigmatic eschatological reversal: what is most dead in the created order will be made most alive by the flow from YHWH's sanctuary.
47.9: “Every living creature that swarms will live; there will be very many fish. For these waters go there and make the sea fresh” — the ecological transformation is total and immediate. “Very many fish” (dagah rabbah meod) — fish abundance was a primary indicator of divine blessing in the ancient Near East; the Nile's fish were Egypt's food staple (Num 11:5: the Israelites in the wilderness remembered the fish they ate in Egypt). The Dead Sea transformed into a fish-teeming body of water is the biological reversal of the curse — where YHWH was absent (the Dead Sea region is associated with Sodom and the cities of the plain, Gen 14:3; 19:24-29), life will flourish through his presence.
47.10: En-gedi (Ein Gedi, “spring of the young goat”) is the famous oasis on the western shore of the Dead Sea, currently one of the few natural freshwater springs in the Judean desert. It was known in biblical times for its vineyards (Song of Songs 1:14) and was a refuge for David when fleeing Saul (1 Sam 24:1). En-eglaim is probably modern Ain Feshkha, near the northern end of the Dead Sea. Fishermen standing along the entire length of the shore from one famous spring to another — from south to north — is the “from Dan to Beersheba” formula applied to the Dead Sea: totality expressed through named endpoints. “Like the fish of the Great Sea” — the Mediterranean was the richest fishing body of water the ancient Israelites knew; the Dead Sea will match its abundance.
47.11: “Its swamps and marshes will not become fresh; they will be left for salt” — even in the eschatological vision, a qualification remains. Some areas of standing water at the margins will not be freshened — they will remain as salt-producing zones. Salt in the OT is the preservative of covenant (Num 18:19: “a covenant of salt forever before the LORD”; 2 Chr 13:5: the Davidic covenant is a “covenant of salt”), the required accompaniment of every grain offering (Lev 2:13), and a practical necessity for preserving food. The preservation of salt-producing marshes in the eschatological order reflects a realism that anticipates Revelation's structure: the new creation is genuinely new but does not abolish all structures of the present order. Some things persist as they are because they are good.
47.12: “On the banks of the river, on both sides, will grow all kinds of trees for food. Their leaves will not wither, nor their fruit fail; they will bear fresh fruit every month, because the water for them flows from the sanctuary. Their fruit will be for food, and their leaves for healing” — this verse closes a canonical arc that opens in Genesis 2:9 (tree of life in the garden, bearing fruit; the river flowing from Eden) and closes in Revelation 22:2 (“the tree of life with its twelve kinds of fruit, yielding its fruit each month. The leaves of the tree were for the healing of the nations”). The specific verbal parallels between Ezek 47:12 and Rev 22:2 are not coincidental: John is citing Ezekiel explicitly, identifying the eschatological river-and-trees as the garden-of-Eden restoration realized in the new Jerusalem. Monthly fruit rather than annual harvest signals perpetual, unceasing abundance — the agricultural cycle of seasons and scarcity replaced by the continuous fruitfulness of the divine economy. Leaves for healing extend the trees' benefit beyond nutrition to restoration: even decay and disease are reversed.
47.13: The land-boundary section begins with the patriarchal formula: “Thus says the Lord GOD: This is the boundary by which you shall divide the land as an inheritance among the twelve tribes of Israel.” Joseph receives two portions (via Manasseh and Ephraim) — this honors the double-portion given to Joseph by Jacob (Gen 48:5-6, 22), which was the inheritance right normally belonging to the firstborn. Reuben (the actual firstborn) lost his double-portion through his sin (Gen 49:3-4; 1 Chr 5:1-2). Joseph's double portion in the eschatological allotment maintains this ancient honor permanently.
47.14: “I raised my hand and swore to give it to your fathers; this land shall fall to you as your inheritance” — the divine oath (nasa yad, raising the hand = oath-gesture in ANE legal tradition) to the patriarchs is the ultimate basis for the eschatological land allotment. The patriarchal promises are repeatedly described as sworn oaths (Gen 12:7; 15:18; 22:16-17; 26:3; 28:13; Exod 6:8; Deut 1:8). The phrase “this land shall fall to you” (venafal halakhem haaretz benahalah) uses the term for the allocation of portions by lot — as if the eschatological land-assignment is itself a cosmic lottery superintended by YHWH. “Equally, one share as well as another” — the eschatological order is characterized by absolute equity: no tribe receives more or better land than another.
47.15: The northern boundary begins at the Great Sea (Mediterranean) and runs northeast toward Lebo-hamath (the entrance to the Syrian city of Hamath on the Orontes River). Lebo-hamath was the traditional northern ideal-boundary of the Promised Land (Num 34:8; 1 Kings 8:65; Amos 6:14). Under David and Solomon, Israel reached this northern extent during the empire's greatest expansion; under most other kings, the actual northern border fell considerably short. The eschatological vision restores the maximum promised extent — not as a correction to history but as the fulfillment of the patriarchal boundaries.
47.16: Berothah and Sibraim are locations on the border between the Aramean kingdoms of Damascus and Hamath — a frontier region roughly corresponding to modern southern Syria/northern Lebanon. Hauran is the fertile volcanic plateau east of the Sea of Galilee (modern Jebel Druze area of southern Syria), known for its rich basaltic soil and agricultural productivity. The boundary description uses real place-names to establish that the eschatological vision is geographically anchored, not purely symbolic — the land promised is a specific piece of the earth, with named borders that could in principle be drawn on a map.
47.17: Hazar-enan (“enclosure of springs”) is the northeastern corner of the boundary — probably in the Damascus region. “The northern border of Damascus, with the border of Hamath beyond” — this establishes that the eschatological allotment includes territory that was previously Aramean, not Israelite. The inclusion of Damascus's northern border implies that even formerly enemy territory is absorbed into the restored Israel — the eschatological boundaries exceed the historical Solomonic boundaries. The vision is not merely restorative but transformative.
47.18: The eastern boundary runs along the Jordan River from Hauran south to the Dead Sea (“eastern sea”). The Jordan as the eastern boundary reflects the traditional Israelite self-understanding: the Transjordanian territories (Reuben, Gad, half-Manasseh) were allocated by Moses (Num 32) but were always understood as the outer edge of the inheritance. In Ezekiel's vision, the Jordan is the boundary — meaning the Transjordanian territories are not included in the twelve-tribe allotment. The twelve tribes are arranged west of the Jordan, the land of the promise, with the Jordan forming the definitive eastern edge.
47.19: The southern boundary runs from Tamar (near the southern end of the Dead Sea, probably modern Ain Husb) westward to Meribah-kadesh (Kadesh-barnea, the wilderness oasis where Israel spent time in the forty years) and then along the Brook of Egypt (nahal Mitzrayim) to the Great Sea. The Brook of Egypt is the Wadi el-Arish, which runs across the Sinai peninsula to the Mediterranean near el-Arish — the traditional southwestern border of Canaan and the historical boundary between Egypt and Israel's territory (1 Kings 8:65). The southern boundary follows the same natural features that historically separated Israel's territory from the Egyptian sphere of influence.
47.20: The western boundary is simply the Great Sea (Mediterranean) — the natural western limit of the known Levantine world. Running “to a point opposite the entrance of Hamath” establishes continuity with the northern boundary (Lebo-hamath) on the coastal side. The four boundaries together (Mediterranean west, Lebanon/Syria north, Jordan east, Sinai south) describe a territory corresponding to the maximum promises of the Abrahamic covenant (Gen 15:18: “from the river of Egypt to the great river, the river Euphrates”) but implementing them through the specific Mosaic boundaries of Num 34 rather than the wider Euphrates-to-Egypt extent.
47.21: “You shall divide this land among yourselves according to the tribes of Israel” — the allocation is plural and communal: not a single monarch's territory but a twelve-tribe inheritance with each tribe having its own defined portion. The tribal structure of the eschatological vision contrasts with the monarchic centralization that preceded the exile. Post-exilic restoration visions (also in Isa 11:12-13; Jer 31:31; and Ezek 37:15-22) consistently envision the twelve-tribe covenant structure renewed rather than replaced by a single kingdom — the return to the tribal covenant form is itself a theological statement about the nature of YHWH's governance.
47.22: “You shall allot it as an inheritance for yourselves and for the resident aliens who live among you and have had children among you. They shall be to you as native-born Israelites; they shall be allotted an inheritance among the tribes of Israel” — this is the most remarkable provision in the eschatological land-allotment. Mosaic law carefully distinguished native Israelites (ezrach) from resident aliens (ger): the ger was protected (Exod 22:21; Deut 10:19) and could participate in the Passover if circumcised (Exod 12:48) but did not own tribal land. Ezekiel's eschatological vision removes this distinction: aliens with children in the community are “as native-born” (k'ezrach) and receive tribal inheritance allotments. This provision is the OT's clearest eschatological anticipation of Gentile inclusion in covenant inheritance — Paul's argument in Eph 2:12-13 (“you who once were far off have been brought near”) and Gal 3:29 (“if you are Christ's then you are Abraham's offspring, heirs according to promise”) are the Christological fulfillment of this verse.
47.23: “In whatever tribe the sojourner resides, there you shall assign his inheritance” — the alien's residence determines their tribal affiliation in the eschatological order. The principle is spatial-relational: belonging is determined by where one has put down roots and built family, not by ethnic descent. This is a striking inversion of the Deuteronomic concern to distinguish Israel from the nations (Deut 7:1-6; 23:3-8): in the eschatological order, the residence-and-family criterion for inheritance replaces the birth-and-genealogy criterion. The practical wisdom is social integration: aliens who have lived among a tribe and raised children there are embedded in its community; the inheritance only confirms what social reality has already established.

48.1: The tribal allotment of ch.48 follows the sacred district placement established in 45:1-8 and 48:8-20. Dan is assigned the northernmost strip — from the road of Hethlon (the route to the Hamath entrance) to Hazar-enan. Dan's placement in the extreme north is theologically charged: Dan was the tribe most associated with idolatry in the northern kingdom. Jeroboam set one of his two golden calves at the city of Dan (1 Kings 12:29-30), and the shrine there was condemned throughout the prophets (Amos 8:14; Hos 8:5-6). Placing Dan at the farthest remove from the sanctuary (in the northernmost strip, seven tribes away from the central sacred district) could be read as honorary distance — a restored but still distinct position — or as simple geometric logic. The Revelation 7 list of the 144,000 conspicuously omits Dan (replaced by Manasseh), suggesting early Christian tradition was aware of Dan's ambiguous status.
48.2: Asher is allocated the second strip from the north, adjacent to Dan. In the historical tribal allotments (Joshua 19:24-31), Asher received territory along the Phoenician coast, north of Carmel. Asher was criticized in the Song of Deborah for remaining at the sea while Israel went to war (Judg 5:17). In the eschatological allotment, Asher occupies interior land rather than coastal territory — the arrangement is east-to-west strips without the geographical features (coastlines, mountain ranges) that determined the historical allotments. The uniform “from the east side to the west side” formula applied to each tribe reflects the eschatological equality of the distribution.
48.3: Naphtali, historically in the upper Jordan valley and the Galilee region (Joshua 19:32-39), receives the third strip. Naphtali was praised in the Song of Deborah (Judg 5:18: “a people who risked their lives to the death”) and was the region where Jesus began his Galilean ministry — Matthew 4:13-16 cites Isa 9:1-2 (“land of Zebulun and Naphtali ... the people dwelling in darkness have seen a great light”) as fulfilled in Jesus's move to Capernaum. In the eschatological vision, Naphtali occupies a strip in the northern distribution without the specific Galilean geography of its historical allotment.
48.4: Manasseh, the half-tribe (one of Joseph's two sons), receives the fourth strip — the only tribe in the northern seven that is half of a double-portion. In the historical allotments, the tribe of Manasseh was split between Cisjordan and Transjordan; in Ezekiel's vision, the Transjordanian territory is not part of the allotment (the Jordan is the eastern boundary, 47:18), so Manasseh receives a single western strip alongside the other tribes. The Joseph-double-portion is expressed through Ephraim and Manasseh each receiving their own full strip — so Joseph effectively gets two strips (vv. 4-5) as promised.
48.5: Ephraim, the preeminent tribe of the northern kingdom and the tribe through which Jacob's blessing flowed (Gen 48:19-20: “his offspring shall become a multitude of nations”), receives the fifth strip. Ephraim's historical prominence — the northern kingdom was often called “Ephraim” in the prophets (Hos 4:17; 5:3; 13:1; Isa 7:2, 5, 8-9; 11:13) — gives its inclusion in the eschatological list significance: the divided kingdom (Israel/Ephraim in the north; Judah in the south) is reunited in the twelve-tribe allotment. Ezek 37:15-22 had already prophesied the re-uniting of the two sticks (Judah + Joseph/Ephraim) into one — the tribal allotment enacts this reunion spatially.
48.6: Reuben, Jacob's firstborn who lost the birthright through his sin with Bilhah (Gen 35:22; 49:3-4), receives the sixth strip — just north of the sacred district, adjacent to Judah. In the historical allotments, Reuben was given Transjordanian territory (Num 32; Joshua 13:15-23). The eschatological allotment places Reuben in Cisjordan (west of the Jordan), in the main tribal body. The forfeited birthright status (which passed to Joseph/Judah in the historical arrangement) does not bar Reuben from the eschatological inheritance — the restoration is genuinely restorative, not merely recapitulating historical rewards and penalties.
48.7: Judah receives the seventh strip, immediately north of the sacred district — the most honored position among the seven northern tribes. The positioning is not accidental: as the tribe of the Davidic covenant and the tribe through which the messianic promise runs (Gen 49:10: “the scepter shall not depart from Judah”), Judah's adjacency to the sacred district honors its covenant role. Judah is separated from the sanctuary only by the sacred district itself and flanks it directly — closer to the divine center than any other tribe. The southern side of the sacred district is flanked by Benjamin (v.23), the other tribe of the southern kingdom, creating a Judah-Benjamin bracket around the sanctuary.
48.8: The sacred district (terumah) is twenty-five thousand cubits wide (east-west) and in breadth matching the tribal strips — approximately twelve kilometers square. The sanctuary (miqdash) is in the midst of the sacred district, centered both east-west and north-south. This centering is architecturally and theologically fundamental: the temple is not at the edge of the sacred territory but at its exact center, equidistant from all borders. The same principle governs Ezekiel's entire city vision: the holy center radiates outward in equal measure in all directions. The city of Jerusalem in Ezekiel's vision does not approach the temple from one direction but circles it equally from all sides.
48.9: The “portion set apart for the LORD” (hatrumah asher tarimu laYHWH) has specific dimensions: twenty-five thousand cubits long and twenty thousand cubits wide. The distinction between the “holy portion” and the “city's property” (v.15) organizes the central territory into three horizontal strips: priests (25,000 × 10,000, with the sanctuary), Levites (25,000 × 10,000), and common use / city (25,000 × 5,000). The width-progression (10,000 priests, 10,000 Levites, 5,000 common) reflects a concentric holiness: the innermost strip contains the most sacred, the city strip is the least sacred within the sacred district.
48.10: The priests' strip (25,000 × 10,000 cubits) has the sanctuary of the LORD at its center. The Zadokite priests are not named here but in v.11; this verse establishes the geographic fact before the identifying qualification. The measurement of the priestly strip as wider than the city strip (10,000 vs. 5,000 cubits) reflects the primacy of the priestly-sacred over the civic-common within the central district. The same hierarchical logic governs the wilderness camp arrangement (Num 2-3): the Levites surround the tabernacle; the priests are closest; the tribes are arrayed by standard; the holy things are at the center.
48.11: “The consecrated priests, the sons of Zadok, who kept my charge and did not go astray when the people of Israel went astray, as the Levites did” — this verse is the eschatological vindication of the Zadokite priestly claim. Zadok was the priest who anointed Solomon (1 Kings 1:39) and whose line served as high priests throughout the First Temple period and into the Second Temple (the Oniad high priests traced their lineage to Zadok until the Maccabean period). The contrast with the Levites “who went astray” refers to Ezek 44:10-14: the Levitical priests who served at the bamot (high places) and participated in Israel's idolatry were demoted from sanctuary service. The Zadokites maintained temple-purity. The Dead Sea Scrolls community called themselves “sons of Zadok” (1QS 5:2; 9:14) — claiming Zadokite priestly legitimacy against the Hasmonean and Herodian high priests they regarded as usurpers.
48.12: “A special allotment from the holy district — a most holy section, adjoining the Levites' territory” — the Zadokite priests' strip is distinguished as qodesh qodashim (most holy), the same category as the temple's inner sanctum. The entire priestly strip shares in the holiness of the sanctuary at its center. “Adjoining the Levites' territory” places the Levites in the strip between the priests and the city: the holiness-gradient (priests → Levites → common) is geographically expressed in the three-strip arrangement of the sacred district.
48.13: The Levites receive a strip identical in dimensions to the priests (25,000 × 10,000) — the same size allocation honors their service even though they were demoted from sanctuary duty (Ezek 44:10-14). The same priestly dignity of service is maintained; only the specific function changes. The equal dimensions refuse to create a permanent underclass within the sacred ministry: the Levites are limited in sanctuary access but equal in spatial provision. This balance between accountability (the demotion stands) and grace (equal allocation) reflects the eschatological order's refusal to either excuse sin or permanently exclude the chastened.
48.14: “They shall not sell or exchange any of it; they shall not alienate this prime portion of the land, for it is holy to the LORD” — the inalienability of the sacred portion is the final fulfillment of the Jubilee principle. Lev 25:23 established the theological basis: “the land shall not be sold in perpetuity, for the land is mine. For you are strangers and sojourners with me.” In the Jubilee system, land alienated through poverty returned to its original owner every fiftieth year — a temporary mechanism for an eternal truth. In the eschatological order, the most holy section is permanently inalienable: no sale, no exchange, no alienation. The land of YHWH's temple is held in trust, not owned, and the trust is permanent.
48.15: The remaining strip of 5,000 × 25,000 cubits is for “common use” (chol — not holy but not ritually impure) — for the city, housing, and open land (migrash, pastureland outside the walls). The city occupies the center of this common strip, flanked by agricultural land on east and west (v.18). This arrangement separates the sacred district (where the sanctuary is) from the civic district (where people live and work) while both remain within the central allotment. The separation of sacred and civic within the same central zone reflects a nuanced theology: the sacred does not dominate all space, but the civic does not displace the sacred either — they coexist in ordered distinction.
48.16: The city's dimensions — 4,500 cubits on each side — form a perfect square (approximately 2.25 km per side, or about 5 km² total area). The perfect square reflects the same mathematical ordering that characterizes the entire temple complex (chs. 40-43): YHWH's order is expressed in geometric precision. The pre-exilic Jerusalem was irregularly shaped and topographically constrained (the narrow ridge between the Kidron and Hinnom valleys); the eschatological city is unconstrained by topography and expresses its idealized form in perfect symmetry.
48.17: Open land (“pastureland”, migrash) of 250 cubits surrounds the city on all four sides — a buffer zone between the city walls and the agricultural land. The migrash was a standard feature of Levitical cities in the Mosaic legislation (Num 35:1-5: each Levitical city had 1,000 cubits of migrash around it). The eschatological city follows the same spatial convention — the city-migrash-farmland sequence creates a gradient from urban to rural that mirrors the sacred-district gradient (most holy → holy → common) in the upper zone.
48.18: The remaining length of the common-use strip — 10,000 cubits on each side of the city — is agricultural land whose produce is food for the city workers (avodei hair). This is the civic economy of the eschatological city: the surrounding agricultural zone sustains the urban population. The “workers of the city” do not live off tribute from subject peoples but from the productivity of their own land — a self-sustaining community rather than an extractive empire. The economic structure of the eschatological city thus reverses the Babylonian imperial model (where conquered peoples' surplus sustained the urban population of Babylon).
48.19: “The workers of the city, from all the tribes of Israel, shall cultivate it” — the agricultural workers come from all twelve tribes, not just from one region. This pan-tribal participation in the city's food production dissolves the tribal-territorial separation that characterized the historical arrangements: all tribes contribute to the city's sustenance, and the city belongs to all tribes equally. The city is not Jerusalem-as-Judah's-capital but Jerusalem as all-Israel's city — the eschatological expression of the principle in v.35 that the city's name is YHWH's presence, not a tribal possession.
48.20: The entire central allotment — 25,000 × 25,000 cubits — forms a perfect square when the sacred district (priests + Levites + city) is combined. The symmetry is explicit: “you shall set apart the holy portion together with the city's property” — they form a unified square. This 25,000 × 25,000 cubit square (approximately 144 km²) is the geometric center of the land, with the sanctuary at its exact center. Revelation's new Jerusalem is a perfect cube/square (Rev 21:16: “the city lies foursquare, its length the same as its width”) — the same mathematical perfection expressing divine order.
48.21: The prince's territory flanks the sacred district on both sides — from the eastern edge of the 25,000 × 25,000 square to the Jordan (eastern border) and from its western edge to the Great Sea (western border). The prince occupies the full width of the tribal strip on both sides of the sacred district without encroaching on the district itself. This is the constitutional structure of Ezekiel's eschatological monarchy: the prince has extensive territory but is explicitly excluded from the sacred zone. Ezek 43:7-8 had condemned the pre-exilic kings who placed their palace “alongside my sanctuary, with only a wall between me and them.” The eschatological arrangement rectifies this by placing a substantial open territory between the prince's land and the sanctuary.
48.22: The boundary details in v.22 clarify that the Levites' and city's portions within the central square are not part of the prince's territory — only the flanking land east and west of the square belongs to the prince. “Between the border of Judah and the border of Benjamin” establishes the vertical position of the central district: Judah to the north and Benjamin to the south, the two tribes of the southern kingdom, bracket the sacred district. This positioning honors the historical fact that Jerusalem was located on the border between Judah and Benjamin (Josh 15:63; 18:28) — even in the eschatological arrangement, the traditional tribal boundaries inform the layout.
48.23: Benjamin receives the first strip south of the sacred district — the position corresponding to Judah's position north of it. Just as Judah is the first northern tribe and is adjacent to the sacred district, Benjamin is the first southern tribe and adjacent to it. The Judah-Benjamin bracketing of the sanctuary continues the historical connection: these two tribes were historically inseparable from Jerusalem and its temple, and in the eschatological arrangement they remain the sanctuary's nearest neighbors. Benjamin's position of honor (immediately adjacent to the sanctuary, south side) corresponds to the tribe's historical identification with the temple mount — the temple was technically within Benjamin's tribal territory according to some boundary-readings (Josh 18:16, 28).
48.24: Simeon receives the second strip south of the sacred district. Simeon was historically one of the most disadvantaged tribes — Jacob's blessing called him scattered in Israel (Gen 49:7) because of his violence at Shechem (Gen 34:25-31), and Simeon's tribal allotment was absorbed within Judah's territory (Joshua 19:1-9) rather than being a separate region. In the eschatological allotment, Simeon receives its own equal strip, undoing the historical dispossession. The restoration of the historically diminished tribe is part of the eschatological pattern: those who were least honored in history receive full honor in the restored order.
48.25: Issachar, historically located in the Jezreel valley (Joshua 19:17-23), receives the third strip south. In the Song of Deborah, Issachar is praised for going into battle (Judg 5:15: “Issachar's princes were with Deborah”). The Chronicler credits the sons of Issachar with “understanding of the times, to know what Israel ought to do” (1 Chr 12:32) — wisdom and discernment were their tribal characterization. In the eschatological vision, their strip occupies the same horizontal position as all others: equal in size, equal in value.
48.26: Zebulun, historically adjacent to Issachar in the lower Galilee region (Joshua 19:10-16), receives the fourth strip south. Zebulun and Naphtali (already placed in the northern third strip) were the tribes of Galilee — the same region Isaiah predicted would see a great light (Isa 9:1-2) and Matthew identified as the location of Jesus's ministry (Matt 4:15-16). In the eschatological allotment they are separated: Naphtali in the north and Zebulun in the south of the sacred district, creating a geographic symmetry around the center.
48.27: Gad receives the southernmost strip — the last tribe before the southern boundary of Tamar and the Brook of Egypt. Historically, Gad occupied Transjordanian territory (the Gilead region, Num 32; Joshua 13:24-28). The eschatological arrangement places Gad in Cisjordan, in the southern extremity — a geographic repositioning that integrates the historically peripheral tribe into the main tribal body. Jacob's blessing (Gen 49:19: “raiders shall raid Gad, but he shall raid at their heels”) characterized Gad as a border-warrior tribe; placement at the southern border of the eschatological allotment may preserve a faint echo of this border-guardian role.
48.28: The southern boundary of Gad's strip runs from Tamar to Meribah-kadesh (Kadesh-barnea) and along the Brook of Egypt (Wadi el-Arish) to the Great Sea — exactly the southern boundary specified in 47:19. The recapitulation of the boundary at Gad's southern edge confirms that the tribal distribution fills the land completely and precisely, with no gaps and no overflows: the southernmost tribe's southern border is the land's southern border. The mathematical precision of the eschatological allotment — equal strips, symmetric boundaries, centered sanctuary — reflects the ordered perfection of the restored creation.
48.29: “This is the land that you shall allot as an inheritance among the tribes of Israel, and these are their portions, declares the Lord GOD” — the land-allocation oracle is formally closed with the double confirmation formula: declarative statement (“this is the land”) and divine speech-marker (“declares the Lord GOD”). The allocation is both human (“you shall allot”) and divine (“declares the Lord GOD”) — the tribes receive their portions by human process, but the legitimacy and finality of the allocation is grounded in YHWH's declaration. This combination of human agency and divine authority mirrors the Jubilee structure: human land-transactions are valid, but YHWH's prior claim and final word set their boundaries.
48.30: The city's gates are introduced — “these shall be the exits of the city” (veeleh totzaot hair). The four walls, each measuring 4,500 cubits, have three gates each, totaling twelve gates for the twelve tribes. The “exits” (totzaot) word implies that the gates are named for the direction the traveler goes out toward each tribe's territory: through the Reuben gate, one heads toward Reuben's strip; through the Dan gate, toward the northernmost territory. The city gates function as a directional map of the whole land, with the city at the center and the tribes radiating outward through their respective gates.
48.31: North wall gates: Reuben, Judah, Levi. The inclusion of Levi among the gate-names is significant — Levi has no tribal territory strip (the Levites' portion is within the central sacred district), but Levi receives a gate. This honors the tribe's priestly identity: even without a land allotment, Levi has a place in the city's gates. Revelation 21:12-13 cites these twelve gates directly: “the wall of the city had twelve gates ... on the gates the names of the twelve tribes of the sons of Israel. On the east three gates, on the north three gates, on the south three gates, and on the west three gates.” The gate-arrangement is one of the most directly cited Ezekielian elements in the NT's eschatological vision.
48.32: East wall gates: Joseph, Benjamin, Dan. Joseph is named (rather than Ephraim + Manasseh separately) — presumably because the full double-portion would require more than one gate name, and the gate system requires exactly three per wall. Alternatively, naming Joseph as one gate honors the tribal patriarch rather than sub-dividing. Dan appears on the east wall — the tribe placed at the northern extremity of the land has a gate on the east wall. The gate placement does not strictly correspond to where each tribe's territory lies; the arrangement likely balances the distribution of names across the four walls.
48.33: South wall gates: Simeon, Issachar, Zebulun. These three tribes had their strips in the southern portion of the central allotment (vv. 24-26), so their south-wall gate placement corresponds roughly to their territorial position. The south wall's three names are all Leah-tribes (sons of Jacob by Leah) — as are the north wall's Reuben and Judah (Levi is also Leah's son). The east wall has Joseph (Rachel's son), Dan (Bilhah's son), and Benjamin (Rachel's son). The west wall has Gad (Zilpah), Asher (Zilpah), and Naphtali (Bilhah). The matriarchal mixing across walls reflects the eschatological dissolution of the hierarchies among Jacob's sons and their mothers — in the restored city, all twelve gates are equal in dignity.
48.34: West wall gates: Gad, Asher, Naphtali. These are the sons of Jacob's concubines Zilpah (Gad, Asher) and Bilhah (Naphtali) — historically considered lower in status than the sons of Leah and Rachel. Yet in the eschatological city, these sons have gates of equal dignity on the city wall. The book of Jubilees and later midrashic tradition wrestled with the status of Jacob's twelve sons; Ezekiel's gate-arrangement refuses any distinction: twelve equal gates, twelve equal tribes, twelve equal positions in the city of God.
48.35: “The circumference of the city shall be eighteen thousand cubits. And the name of the city from that day on shall be: The LORD Is There” (YHWH-Shammah). The final verse of Ezekiel is the theological climax toward which the entire book has been moving. The book's crisis is the departure of YHWH's glory (kavod) from the temple in chs. 10-11: the Shekinah rose from the cherubim, paused at the temple threshold, went to the eastern gate, and then departed eastward from the city (10:18-19; 11:22-23), as if YHWH himself went into exile with his people. The book's restoration vision is the reversal of this departure: the glory returns from the east through the eastern gate (43:1-5), fills the temple, and declares permanent residence (43:7-9). The city's final name — YHWH-Shammah — is not a prediction of the future but a declaration of permanent present reality: YHWH Is There, not “was there” or “will be there.” The divine presence that departed in judgment has returned in grace, and its presence defines the city's identity for all time. The OT divine-name tradition (YHWH-Nissi, Exod 17:15; YHWH-Shalom, Judg 6:24; YHWH-Tsidkenu, Jer 23:6; YHWH-Raah, Gen 22:14) reaches its apex here: the most comprehensive divine name is not an attribute but presence itself — YHWH is there, and that is sufficient.
